We introduce ForecastBench-Sim, a simulated-world forecasting benchmark built on game rollouts from Freeciv, a turn-based strategy game modelled on the Civilization series. Forecasters receive a fixed world report (a structured snapshot of the current game state) and answer questions about hidden future states; the benchmark then continues the simulation and scores forecasts. Because the world is simulated, the same setup can generate continuous or binary forecasting questions at arbitrary time horizons, paired intervention worlds for conditional or causal questions, and resolved examples of rare or disruptive outcomes. We describe the benchmark pipeline, question families, scoring protocol, and release artifacts, and report validation slices from model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot. ForecastBench-Sim is intended to complement real-world forecasting benchmarks by providing controlled, immediately resolvable tasks for studying probabilistic reasoning under dynamic world states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ForecastBench-Sim, a forecasting benchmark using game simulations to study probabilistic reasoning.
AI.motivation: Real-world forecasting benchmarks have limitations such as slow resolution, rare tail events, and difficulty scoring counterfactuals.
AI.method: The authors use Freeciv game rollouts to simulate worlds, enabling the creation of structured questions about future states and immediate scoring of the forecasts.
AI.result: They validate the benchmark with model evaluations and an anonymized human pilot, demonstrating its potential usefulness.
AI.conclusion: ForecastBench-Sim offers a controlled environment for studying probabilistic reasoning and complements real-world benchmarks by overcoming real-world constraints.

title: SWE-Future: Forecast-Conditioned Data Synthesis for Future-Oriented Software Engineering Agents
summary: Realistic coding-agent benchmarks often replay public GitHub issues and pull requests, making them vulnerable to overlap with model pretraining, fine-tuning, synthetic-data generation, or benchmark-driven model selection. Fully synthetic tasks avoid direct historical replay, but can drift away from real repository needs. We propose SWE-Future, a forecast-conditioned data synthesis method for future-oriented coding tasks. Given a forecast snapshot at time $T_0$, the method uses only pre-$T_0$ repository evidence to forecast future feature implementation/enhancement, bugfix, and refactor task families. We first validate this forecasting step retrospectively: after forecasts are fixed, later pull requests are used only to measure whether the predicted task families match future repository work. In an 80-repository study, the forecaster achieves 58.1\% future-work relevance under the main semantic matching metric. We then use validated forecast families as conditioning signals to synthesize a 200-task coding-agent dataset across 61 repositories from a task-generation snapshot, rather than replaying the later pull requests used for validation. SWE-Future shows that repository-evolution forecasts can guide realistic, future-oriented coding-task synthesis while reducing direct dependence on historical pull-request replay.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SWE-Future, a method that synthesizes coding-agent tasks based on repository evolution forecasts to avoid issues from direct historical pull-request replay.
AI.motivation: Common coding-agent benchmarks rely on public GitHub data, but this approach risks overlap with model training data or diverging from actual repository needs.
AI.method: SWE-Future uses a forecast-conditioned data synthesis method that relies on pre-snapshot repository evidence to predict and synthesize future coding tasks, later validating these forecasts with pull-request data.
AI.result: In an 80-repository study, the forecasting model demonstrated 58.1% relevance in predicting future repository tasks, leading to a 200-task dataset synthesized from 61 repositories.
AI.conclusion: SWE-Future enables realistic and future-oriented coding-task synthesis while mitigating the reliance on historical task replay and potential overlaps.

title: VISUALSKILL: Multimodal Skills for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents (CUAs) approach human-level performance on standardised benchmarks but still struggle on long-horizon tasks and unseen software. Existing skill libraries address this with reusable skills, but represent the skill artifact as text only, despite the visual nature of GUI interaction. We propose VISUALSKILL: a hierarchical multimodal skill, tailored to each target application and organised as a central index over per-topic files, which the agent consumes through a load_topic MCP tool that fetches the relevant topic's text and figures on demand. We construct each skill with a two-stage pipeline that combines authored documentation with live-application UI exploration. On two CUA benchmarks, CUA-World and OSExpert-Eval, a Claude Code CLI agent backed by Claude Opus 4.6 reaches an average score of 0.456 with VISUALSKILL, a +15.3 point absolute lift over the no-skill baseline (0.303). Against a matched text-only skill that is generated from the same source content and differs from VISUALSKILL only in modality, VISUALSKILL yields a further +8.3 point absolute gain over the matched text-only skill (0.373 vs. 0.456), providing direct evidence that retaining visual figures in the skill artifact, rather than verbalizing them away, helps the agent both identify UI elements and verify workflow state after each action. Our code is available at https://github.com/XMHZZ2018/VisualSkills.

AI.tldr: VISUALSKILL is a hierarchical multimodal skill framework that significantly improves computer-use agents' performance on GUI interactions by incorporating both visual and text elements into skills.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents (CUAs) perform well in standard benchmarks but fail in long-horizon tasks and unfamiliar software. Existing skill libraries exclude visual elements despite the predominance of GUI-driven interactions.
AI.method: The VISUALSKILL framework organizes skills using a hierarchical, multimodal format. Skills are created with a two-stage approach combining documentation and live UI exploration. The agent accesses visual figures and text dynamically through an indexing system.
AI.result: VISUALSKILL improves performance on two benchmarks: CUA-World and OSExpert-Eval, achieving a score of 0.456 compared to a no-skill baseline of 0.303. It also outperforms text-only equivalent skills by an additional 8.3 points.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating visual elements into skills significantly enhances an agent's ability to interact with GUIs, suggesting the importance of multimodal approaches for complex software tasks.

title: As You Wish: Mission Planning with Formal Verification using LLMs in Precision Agriculture
summary: Though robotic systems are now being commercialized and deployed in various industries, many of these systems are highly specialized and often require an advanced skill set to operate and ensure they perform as instructed. To mitigate this problem, we recently introduced a mission planner leveraging LLMs to synthesize mission plans in precision agriculture based on mission descriptions provided in natural language. While the system demonstrates impressive performance, it also suffers from the inherent ambiguities of natural language. In this paper, we extend our system to address this issue by introducing multiple feedback loops in the planning architecture that leverage linear temporal logic (LTL) to ensure the mission planning system meets the specifications formulated by the user while still using natural language. To mitigate potential bias, this is achieved by using two different commercial LLMs in charge of the specification and verification subtasks. Through extensive experiments, we highlight the strengths and limitations of integrating mission verification into a fully autonomous pipeline, particularly regarding an LLM's ability to generate valuable LTL formulas, and show how our proposed implementation addresses and solves these challenges.

AI.tldr: The paper enhances a mission planner system for precision agriculture using LLMs by introducing feedback loops with linear temporal logic (LTL) to tackle natural language ambiguities, ensuring mission specifications are met.
AI.motivation: Specialized robotic systems require advanced skills, and their natural language-based mission planners suffer from inherent ambiguities. This work aims to address this limitation.
AI.method: The authors introduce a planning architecture with multiple feedback loops leveraging linear temporal logic (LTL) and use two LLMs for specification and verification tasks to reduce bias.
AI.result: The system integrates mission verification into its pipeline and proves its ability to generate useful LTL formulas through extensive experiments, addressing challenges effectively.
AI.conclusion: The implementation overcomes natural language limitations in autonomous mission planning for precision agriculture, although further improvements may be needed for full deployment.

title: Vines-DB: An RGB image dataset for multi-species ornamental vine segmentation
comment: 7 pages, 1 figure. Source data repository: OSF (DOI: 10.17605/OSF.IO/YJHCK)
summary: The Vines-DB dataset contains 1,218 original high-resolution RGB images of seven ornamental vine species collected under field conditions at the Utah Agricultural Experiment Station's Greenville Research Farm in Logan, Utah, USA. The dataset was generated from 168 individual vine plants that were transplanted in 2022 and photographed repeatedly across multiple months during the 2023 and 2024 growing seasons (July-October). Images were captured with an iPhone 16 Pro equipped with a 48 MP camera between 10:00 AM and 12:00 PM under daylight. Vines were grown on 1.2m x 2.4m trellises and photographed from a distance of 1m against black or white Styrofoam backdrops to improve contrast and reduce background noise. The dataset includes Akebia quinata, Campsis radicans, Hydrangea anomala petiolaris, Lonicera x heckrottii, Campsis x tagliabuana 'Madame Galen', Parthenocissus quinquefolia, and Wisteria floribunda. All original images were manually annotated in Roboflow by trained annotators to produce polygon-based instance segmentation masks for eight classes, including seven species and background. After preprocessing and data augmentation, the working dataset was expanded to 2,307 images for model development and evaluation. The augmented dataset was divided into 2,019 training images, 192 validation images, and 96 test images using stratified sampling to maintain balanced representation. Vines-DB supports the development and evaluation of deep learning models for multi-class instance segmentation in precision horticulture and urban ecology. The dataset enables applications such as automated canopy cover estimation, species identification, and scalable field phenotyping. In addition, repeated monthly imaging of the plants captures temporal variation in canopy development and plant appearance, increasing the dataset's utility for segmentation benchmarking under realistic field conditions.

AI.tldr: The Vines-DB dataset consists of 1,218 high-resolution images of seven vine species, expanded through augmentation and annotated for segmentation tasks. It supports deep learning applications like species identification and canopy cover estimation in horticulture.
AI.motivation: To develop and provide a dataset that facilitates deep learning-driven segmentation and identification of ornamental vine species, aiding precision horticulture and urban ecology tasks.
AI.method: Images were collected over multiple months using a high-resolution iPhone camera, annotated with polygon-based instance segmentation masks, and augmented to expand the dataset for training, validation, and testing of machine learning models.
AI.result: The dataset includes 2,307 images after augmentation, consisting of balanced training (2,019), validation (192), and test (96) sets, enhanced by annotations for benchmarking segmentation models in realistic conditions.
AI.conclusion: Vines-DB is a valuable resource for advancing deep learning methods in horticulture, enabling applications like species differentiation, temporal canopy studies, and field phenotyping.

title: TimeLAVA: Learning-Agnostic Data Valuation for Time Series
summary: Data valuation quantifies the intrinsic quality of individual samples to enable principled data curation, quality control, and robust learning. For time series in critical domains such as healthcare, finance, and industrial monitoring, effective valuation methods are essential yet fundamentally lacking. Existing approaches are either model-dependent, limiting their generalizability, or designed for i.i.d. data and thus fail to capture temporal dependencies, multi-scale patterns, and non-stationary dynamics inherent to sequential data. We introduce TimeLAVA, a learning-agnostic framework that values temporal segments by their marginal contribution to minimizing distributional discrepancy between evaluated and reference data. At its core is a novel Selective Wavelet-based Wasserstein discrepancy combining multi-scale wavelet transforms for temporal localization with unbalanced optimal transport for robustness to distributional shifts. Segment values are efficiently computed via sensitivity analysis without requiring model training and aggregated into point-wise scores. We provide theoretical guarantees linking valuation to model-agnostic generalization and prove bounded sensitivity to outlier contamination. Extensive experiments across anomaly detection, data pruning, and label noise detection demonstrate that TimeLAVA produces significantly more informative value scores than existing methods on diverse real-world datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TimeLAVA, a learning-agnostic framework for valuing temporal segments in time series by minimizing distributional discrepancies, offering improvements over existing methods on real-world tasks such as anomaly detection and label noise detection.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of effective and generalizable data valuation methods for time series in critical domains like healthcare, finance, and industrial monitoring.
AI.method: The authors propose TimeLAVA, which uses a Selective Wavelet-based Wasserstein discrepancy metric to evaluate temporal segments. It leverages sensitivity analysis for efficient valuation and combines wavelet transforms with unbalanced optimal transport to handle multi-scale patterns and distributional shifts.
AI.result: TimeLAVA delivers superior data valuation performance, as demonstrated through experiments on diverse real-world datasets for tasks including anomaly detection, data pruning, and label noise detection.
AI.conclusion: TimeLAVA effectively bridges the gap in generalizable and robust time series valuation methods, offering theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance in real-world applications.

title: Fisher Width: A Geometric Measure of Complexity on Statistical Manifolds
summary:
Gaussian width is a central geometric complexity measure in high-dimensional probability, compressed sensing, convex optimization, and learning theory. It quantifies the average extent of a set along random directions, thereby capturing the effective dimension of constraint sets, hypothesis classes, and descent cones. However, this notion is intrinsically Euclidean. Statistical models instead carry a natural Riemannian geometry induced by the Fisher information metric, where directions are scaled according to statistical distinguishability rather than ambient Euclidean length.
  We introduce Fisher width, a Fisher-geometric analogue of Gaussian width for statistical manifolds. At a parameter point $θ$, Fisher width replaces the Euclidean identity by the local metric tensor $G(θ)^{1/2}$, measuring the Gaussian width of the Fisher-rescaled set. This makes the resulting quantity sensitive to local statistical curvature and invariant under smooth reparameterizations.
  We develop the basic theory of Fisher width, showing that it retains key structural features of Gaussian width, including concentration, metric perturbation stability, and spectral comparison bounds with the Euclidean baseline, while also capturing anisotropic geometric effects invisible to Euclidean measures. As an application, we prove a generalization bound for Fisher-Lipschitz hypothesis classes and propose computable estimators, which we evaluate empirically on MNIST across three model classes.
  Fisher width is to statistical manifolds what Gaussian width is to Euclidean convex bodies. This work lays the foundation for studying complexity and learning on curved statistical manifolds.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Fisher width," a Riemannian geometric analogue of Gaussian width for statistical manifolds, and explores its theory, applications, and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Gaussian width, while effective for Euclidean spaces, does not account for the natural Riemannian geometry of statistical manifolds induced by the Fisher information metric. A new measure is needed to handle statistical manifolds.
AI.method: Fisher width is defined using the Fisher information metric to assess statistical distinguishability, maintaining properties like concentration, stability, and comparison bounds. The authors apply this measure to Fisher-Lipschitz hypothesis classes and test estimators empirically on MNIST.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates theoretical features of Fisher width and empirical results showing its relevance to statistical manifolds. Experimental analysis validates the practical utility of the proposed measure.
AI.conclusion: Fisher width is a foundational tool for complexity analysis on statistical manifolds, bridging gaps between Euclidean and Riemannian perspectives in learning theory.

title: Closed-Form and Constant-Time New-Source Selection for Fault-Tolerant Broadcasting in Dense Gaussian Networks
summary: Fault-tolerant broadcasting in dense Gaussian networks is recovered by re-rooting the broadcast at a new source at maximum graph distance from the faulty nodes. This paper extends the re-rooting framework by replacing its boundary-search source-selection step with a quotient-lattice-aware algebraic construction. The first contribution is a constant-time counting method for valid new sources, formulated as an intersection of two diameter-$k$ boundary sets in the Gaussian quotient. The exact count is obtained by a fixed union of side-pair intervals over nine quotient-lattice copies, giving a closed-form procedure without scanning the network or boundary. The second contribution is a shifted direct selector for two arbitrary faulty nodes. Given faulty nodes $A$ and $B$, the problem is translated to $C=\operatorname{mod}_{G_k}(B-A)$, and the selector finds $P$ satisfying $d(P,0)=d(P,C)=k$. For each of nine quotient-lattice shifts, sixteen signed linear systems are checked. Nonparallel systems are solved via Cramer's rule; parallel systems are handled by interval-endpoint selection. At most $9\times16=144$ shifted sign cases are evaluated, giving $O(1)$ selection under the word-RAM model. Validation reports zero count mismatches over $26{,}623$ tested nodes, $500{,}000$ valid outputs over $500{,}000$ sampled fault pairs, and $40{,}000$ successful re-rooted broadcast trials. The shifted selector achieves a $5.92\times$ speedup over boundary search at $k=200$, remaining stable as $k$ increases. These results make new-source selection algebraic, bounded, and independent of network size.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an optimized, constant-time method for fault-tolerant broadcasting in Gaussian networks by applying algebraic approaches for selecting new broadcast sources.
AI.motivation: To improve the efficiency and scalability of fault-tolerant broadcasting in dense Gaussian networks, especially by addressing the source-selection step during re-rooting from faulty nodes.
AI.method: Introduced a constant-time source-counting method using quotient lattices and algebraic constructs, along with a shifted direct selector for new broadcast source computations under fault scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrated zero mismatches in validation over 26,623 nodes, correctly computed all 500,000 sampled pairs, and achieved a $5.92\times$ speedup in performance compared to traditional methods at $k=200$.
AI.conclusion: The proposed methods make source selection for fault-tolerant broadcasting in Gaussian networks algebraic, efficient, and independent of network size.

title: What Must Generalist Agents Remember?
summary: This paper develops a formal account of what generalist agents must store in memory in order to act near-optimally across multiple environments and goals. It shows that when two domains share an observational bottleneck but require incompatible optimal actions, any uniformly near-optimal policy must induce distinct memory distributions at that bottleneck. The result yields a separation theorem: sufficiently successful agents cannot rely only on current state observations, but must preserve domain-relevant information in memory. The paper further shows that if an agent's memory contains enough information to estimate values for related goals, then that memory can be used to approximately reconstruct the agent's local transition dynamics. Together, these results characterize memory as the substrate that supports domain disambiguation, transition-model reconstruction, and planning for generalist agents.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a formal understanding of memory requirements for generalist agents to act effectively across various environments and goals.
AI.motivation: To explore how generalist agents need memory to overcome limitations of shared information bottlenecks and act optimally in diverse domains.
AI.method: Development of theoretical results and proofs showcasing memory requirements, domain disambiguation, and planning capabilities of agents.
AI.result: Memory enables agents to disambiguate domains, reconstruct transition dynamics, and support planning for value estimation across goals.
AI.conclusion: Generalist agents must have memory to retain domain-relevant information, supporting optimal behavior and adaptability across environments.

title: Toward Semantically-Seeded, Graph-Propagated Impact Analysis Across Software Artifacts: A Vision
summary: When a single software artifact changes - a requirement, a configuration value, or a function - engineers must determine what else is impacted. Existing change-impact-analysis (CIA) tooling tends to rely on one of two signals in isolation: semantic similarity recovered from text (information-retrieval traceability, code search, embeddings), or structural dependency following (call graphs, IDE "find usages", test-impact selection). Each has a characteristic blind spot. A semantically driven tool misses an impacted artifact whose text shares no vocabulary with the change; a structurally driven tool misses artifacts related in meaning but not joined by an edge, and most operate only over code rather than the Requirement-Config-Service-Test chain. We argue for a training-free and interpretable analyzer that fuses both signals over the same embeddings. We model the system as a heterogeneous artifact graph with typed edges recovered by static analysis, compute a semantic prior by cosine similarity to the changed artifact, propagate impact multi-hop with decay over a row-normalized propagation matrix, and blend the two with a single tunable weight lambda. A small but complete proof-of-concept on a payment subsystem (5 labelled change scenarios) shows the mechanism we care about: artifacts with zero textual overlap with the change are still recovered through propagation, and helper functions that propagation alone cannot reach are recovered through the semantic layer. The fusion is the only configuration that covers both blind spots, and lambda acts as an explicit precision/recall control. Drawing on four publicly documented production failures, we argue that the same formulation extends to operational artifacts (images, metrics, dashboards, data schemas) that code-only analysis cannot reach.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method for change-impact analysis (CIA) that fuses semantic similarity and structural dependency signals into a single tool, addressing blind spots in existing techniques.
AI.motivation: Engineers face challenges in identifying impacted artifacts when software changes occur, as existing CIA tools often rely exclusively on semantic or structural signals, each with limitations.
AI.method: The proposed system uses a heterogeneous artifact graph with static analysis, computes semantic similarity via cosine distance, propagates impact multi-hop with decay, and blends semantic and structural signals using a tunable weight lambda.
AI.result: A proof-of-concept test on a payment subsystem demonstrates improved impact recovery, covering artifacts missed by traditional semantic or structural methods.
AI.conclusion: The fusion mechanism effectively addresses blind spots in both semantic and structural CIA methods, showing promise for broader operational artifact analysis beyond code.

title: LLM Parameters for Math Across Languages: Shared or Separate?
comment: 5 pages. Accepted at ACL Student Research Workshop (SRW) 2026. Code: https://github.com/luisavictor/math-across-languages Translated Datasets: https://huggingface.co/math-across-languages Webpage: https://math-across-languages.github.io
summary: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit substantial cross-lingual variation in mathematical reasoning performance, but it remains unclear whether these differences reflect language-specific parameters or a shared mechanism that manifests differently by language. We present a cross-lingual mechanistic analysis of mathematical reasoning in LLMs, enabling us to localize and compare model parameters that support mathematical reasoning across languages. We find that the extracted math-associated parameters exhibit partial cross-lingual overlap, with the strongest overlap concentrated in intermediate model layers. We further observe that English consistently produces the largest set of math-relevant parameters, whereas lower-resource languages reveal smaller sets of relevant parameters. These results suggest that math-related behavior in multilingual LLMs is neither fully language-invariant nor fully language-specific, but instead exhibits partial cross-lingual parameter overlap with systematic language-dependent differences.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how large language models (LLMs) handle mathematical reasoning across multiple languages, finding partial overlap in parameters that vary by language and model layers.
AI.motivation: The study aims to understand whether variations in mathematical reasoning performance across languages in LLMs are due to shared mechanisms or language-specific parameters.
AI.method: The authors conduct a cross-lingual mechanistic analysis, localizing and comparing the parameters in LLMs that facilitate mathematical reasoning.
AI.result: They discover that math-related parameters show partial overlap across languages, with the strongest overlap in intermediate layers of the model. English yields the largest number of relevant parameters, while lower-resource languages exhibit fewer.
AI.conclusion: The findings indicate that while math reasoning in multilingual LLMs is neither fully language-invariant nor language-specific, it reflects a mix of shared, overlapping, and language-dependent parameter configurations.

title: DREAM-Chunk: Reactive Action Chunking with Latent World Model
summary: Action chunking has become a common interface for vision-language-action (VLA) models, enabling low-frequency policy inference to drive high-frequency robot execution. However, once an action chunk is committed, its open-loop execution can be brittle under stochastic dynamics, hardware execution errors, and partial observability. We propose DREAM-Chunk, a test-time scaling method that augments chunking-based policies with a lightweight latent world model, without requiring additional policy fine-tuning. At test time, DREAM-Chunk samples multiple candidate action chunks, rolls out their predicted latent futures, and selects actions from the chunk whose predicted state best matches the observed rollout. In this way, DREAM-Chunk uses additional test-time computation to cover multiple plausible stochastic futures and improve reactivity during long-horizon chunk execution. On the Kinetix benchmark, DREAM-Chunk improves robustness under increasing action noise and benefits from larger candidate sample sizes, especially when demonstrations contain corrective behaviors. We further validate DREAM-Chunk on four manipulation tasks across two robot platforms and two VLA policies under various sources of stochasticity. Across simulation and hardware experiments, DREAM-Chunk improves the robustness of action-chunking policies in stochastic dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DREAM-Chunk, a method to enhance chunking-based policies in vision-language-action (VLA) models by utilizing a latent world model for improved robustness during action execution.
AI.motivation: Action chunking for VLA models faces challenges like execution brittleness due to stochastic dynamics, hardware errors, and partial observability. The paper aims to address this limitation.
AI.method: DREAM-Chunk augments chunking-based policies by sampling multiple candidate action chunks, predicting their latent futures, and selecting the chunk that best matches observed rollouts during test time without needing policy fine-tuning.
AI.result: On the Kinetix benchmark and across four manipulation tasks on two robot platforms, DREAM-Chunk shows improved robustness and reactivity, especially under conditions of stochastic dynamics and action noise.
AI.conclusion: DREAM-Chunk effectively enhances the robustness and adaptability of VLA models during action execution, particularly in unpredictable and noisy environments.

title: Neural Phase Correlation
summary:
Correspondence is fundamentally relational: it seeks the unknown transformation between two observations of a common scene, not the content of either. Yet the dominant learning-based methods do not represent the transformation as a first-class object in the architecture. They encode each image independently and let a learned similarity function or a deep decoder discover the mapping implicitly. Phase correlation is the canonical exception, measuring the inter-image relationship directly in the Fourier domain, but the rigidity of its fixed basis confines it to global translation.
  We introduce a learned generalization of phase correlation that lifts this restriction by learning the basis on which the transformation decomposes. The same algebraic primitive extends to dense non-rigid deformations and to unitary dynamics. On the ACDC cardiac-MRI benchmark the framework matches or exceeds prior published baselines on both registration directions. On CAMUS echocardiography it matches state-of-the-art without auxiliary scoring or adaptive-smoothness mechanisms. Applied to time-evolved wavefunction pairs of the 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator, the same framework recovers the Hermite-function eigenstates and the quantized energy levels of the unknown Hamiltonian from observation pairs alone.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a learned generalization of phase correlation that enables direct measurement of transformations beyond rigid global translations, improving on benchmarks like cardiac MRI and echocardiography and even demonstrating applicability in quantum mechanics.
AI.motivation: Current correspondence methods in computer vision do not treat transformations between observations as fundamental objects and rely on implicit mappings, limiting their flexibility. Traditional phase correlation addresses this relational problem directly but is confined to rigid, global translations.
AI.method: The authors propose a learned generalization of phase correlation by designing a framework that learns a transformation basis. This eliminates the rigidity of fixed bases, extending the technique to complex transformations, including dense non-rigid deformations and unitary dynamics.
AI.result: On the ACDC benchmark for cardiac MRI, the framework achieves state-of-the-art or surpasses existing baselines. On CAMUS echocardiography, it achieves top-tier performance without relying on auxiliary mechanisms. Additionally, it generalizes to quantum mechanics by recovering eigenstates and quantized energy levels of a 1-D quantum harmonic oscillator.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully generalizes phase correlation to a wider range of transformations, achieving competitive results in medical imaging tasks and extending its utility to quantum mechanical phenomena without reliance on traditional rigid assumptions.

title: Kernel of Partition Paths: A Unified Representation for Tree Ensembles
summary: A recent line of work has reframed individual decision trees as linear models on engineered features associated with their splits, opening routes for oracle inequalities and feature-importance reinterpretation, but leaving open the question of what unified geometric object a forest induces when one indexes its feature map by nodes rather than by splits. The present paper studies that object. KPP indexes the feature map by the nodes of the forest, weighted by a path metric that turns each coordinate into a component of a squared-Euclidean path-isometric embedding. KPP unifies four pillars under a single non-diagonal Gram that carries a metric: prediction, exact additive attribution, deterministic Lipschitz robust radius in the KPP metric, and uniform Rademacher risk bounds for regression and classification under fixed, honest, or cross-fit conditioning. All probabilistic guarantees are conditional on the representation and are stated under three explicit conditioning regimes; the robust-radius guarantee is deterministic in the KPP metric rather than in a norm on the raw input. Conjectured fast-rate refinements for both regression and classification are stated as open problems and are not claimed as theorems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KPP, a novel representation of random forest feature maps indexed by nodes and weighted by a path metric, enabling unified geometric interpretations and providing deterministic robust-radius guarantees and probabilistic bounds. Conjectures for fast-rate refinements remain open.
AI.motivation: To address the question of what unified geometric object a forest induces when its feature map is indexed by nodes, building on the reframing of decision trees as linear models.
AI.method: KPP indexes feature maps by forest nodes, applies a path metric for squared-Euclidean path-isometric embedding, and integrates it into non-diagonal Gram structures for unified predictions, attributions, robust-radius guarantees, and Rademacher risk bounds.
AI.result: KPP provides deterministic robust-radius guarantees in its metric, probabilistic guarantees across three conditioning regimes, and integrates multiple aspects under a unified structure, with conjectured fast-rate refinements.
AI.conclusion: KPP offers a novel way to geometrically and probabilistically study decision forests, advancing unified metrics and robustness guarantees while opening avenues for further theoretical investigation.

title: DRIFT: Refining Instruction Data via On-Policy Data Attribution
summary: Optimizing the training data distribution for Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) dictates the capability of Large Language Models (LLMs). While existing data curation methods excel at accelerating training under constrained budgets, they are less suited to elevating the capability upper bound. The challenge here is no longer to identify a smaller subset that preserves performance, but to refine the data distribution toward instances most capable of improving the final model. To address this problem, we explore instance-level data attribution using Influence Functions (IF). We identify that standard IF formulations struggle in this setting due to two structural limitations: a proximity gap caused by off-policy validation targets, and a severe bias towards gradient norm. We propose DRIFT (Data Refinement via On-Policy Influence Functions for Supervised Fine-Tuning). Instead of relying on external reference data, DRIFT utilizes the model's on-policy rollouts as validation targets, which empirically minimizes the parameter proximity gap and better aligns with the local neighborhood assumption of IF. It further applies signed weighting based on trajectory correctness and debiases influence scores against the gradient hacking issue, allowing a small set of validation queries to act as reliable anchors for attributing the full dataset. Experiments on 7B-parameter instruction and reasoning models show that DRIFT consistently raises the performance ceiling on both, outperforming existing data curation baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DRIFT, a method using on-policy Influence Functions to refine data distribution for supervised fine-tuning of Large Language Models, significantly improving their capabilities.
AI.motivation: Existing data curation methods are limited in elevating the capability upper bound of Large Language Models (LLMs). This paper aims to improve the distribution of training instances to enhance the final model's performance.
AI.method: DRIFT employs on-policy rollouts for validation targets, signed weighting based on trajectory correctness, and debiasing techniques to refine the training dataset effectively.
AI.result: DRIFT improves the performance ceiling of 7B-parameter instruction and reasoning models, outperforming traditional data curation techniques.
AI.conclusion: Using DRIFT for instance-level data attribution enables better data refinement and boosts the capability of large language models beyond existing methods.

title: ReMP: Low-Downtime Runtime Model-Parallelism Reconfiguration for LLM Serving
summary: Current large language model (LLM) inference systems universally deploy ultra-large-scale models using a combination of Tensor Parallelism (TP) and Pipeline Parallelism (PP). However, existing systems treat the model parallelism topology as a static configuration that cannot be flexibly adjusted at runtime. This rigid design creates a fundamental contradiction with the dynamically changing inference workloads in real-world scenarios. State-of-the-art systems lack online reconfiguration capabilities and can only switch configurations by restarting the service, resulting in several minutes of service interruption, KV cache loss, and prohibitive recomputation overhead. To address this problem, this paper presents ReMP, a runtime model parallelism reconfiguration framework that supports low downtime. ReMP achieves dynamic adjustment through three key techniques: (1) decoupling the model parallelism topology from runtime state to avoid full service reconstruction; (2) designing a two-dimensional KV cache migration mechanism to preserve reusable cache states after TP/PP changes; and (3) implementing end-to-end online reconfiguration. Experiments demonstrate that ReMP can complete most topology switches within 1-7 seconds on models ranging from 7B to 70B parameters, achieving speedups of tens to over a hundred times compared to the restart approach. Moreover, ReMP significantly outperforms fixed configurations under dynamic workloads, delivering superior performance in terms of TTFT, TPOT, and output throughput.

AI.tldr: ReMP is a framework for runtime model parallelism reconfiguration in LLM inference, reducing downtime and improving performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM inference systems rely on static model parallelism topologies that cannot dynamically adjust to varying workloads, leading to inefficiency and service interruptions.
AI.method: ReMP employs three techniques: decoupling model topology from runtime state, implementing two-dimensional KV cache migration, and enabling end-to-end online reconfiguration.
AI.result: The framework completes topology switches in 1-7 seconds for models of 7B to 70B parameters, significantly reducing downtime and outperforming static configurations in dynamic workloads.
AI.conclusion: ReMP addresses the rigidity of current LLM inference systems by enabling dynamic reconfiguration, making it more suitable for real-world, dynamic workloads.

title: R2D-RL: A RoboCup 2D Soccer Environment for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Robot soccer is a challenging testbed for multi-agent reinforcement learning because it combines partial observability, cooperative and adversarial interaction, sparse rewards, and long-horizon tactical behavior. RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation (RCSS2D) provides a mature robot-soccer platform, but its competition-oriented server-client architecture is difficult to use directly with modern Python-based MARL workflows. We introduce R2D-RL, a reinforcement learning environment that connects RCSS2D and HELIOS-based player clients to a Python MARL interface through shared-memory communication and cycle-level synchronization. R2D-RL supports full-field and scenario-based training with configurable opponents, Base discrete and Hybrid parameterized action spaces, action masks, expected possession value (EPV)-based reward shaping, and parallel execution. We provide front-goal scenarios and an 11-vs-11 full-field benchmark, together with baseline results.

AI.tldr: RoboCup 2D Soccer Simulation (RCSS2D) serves as a testbed for multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), but challenges arise due to its architecture. R2D-RL offers a solution by integrating RCSS2D into Python MARL workflows through shared memory and synchronization.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to improve the accessibility of RCSS2D for MARL research by addressing difficulties with its original competition-oriented architecture.
AI.method: Introduces R2D-RL, a reinforcement learning environment that connects RCSS2D to Python via shared memory communication and cycle-level synchronization, enhancing features like action spaces, reward shaping, and scalability.
AI.result: R2D-RL successfully supports scenario-based and full-field training for robot soccer, with benchmark outcomes provided alongside example scenarios.
AI.conclusion: R2D-RL simplifies the use of RCSS2D for MARL researchers and offers advanced tools to foster progress in robot soccer training and analysis.

title: CAPRA: Scaling Feedback on Software Architecture Deliverables with a Multi-Agent LLM System
comment: Accepted for publication at the 38th International Conference on Software Engineering Education and Training
summary: Automated assessment in software engineering education has advanced significantly for code grading and essay scoring. However, reviewing software architecture deliverables, which requires analyzing structural completeness and requirements traceability, has not yet been fully automated. Applying Large Language Models (LLMs) to this task requires robust architectures to ensure technical feedback is accurate and reliable for students. This paper presents CAPRA (Configurable Architecture Proficiency Report Assessment), a multi-agent LLM system that analyzes software architecture deliverables to generate personalized, template-compliant LaTeX feedback. As a core design choice, CAPRA coordinates multiple specialized agents and employs a Python-based microservice for multi-modal document extraction, utilizing PyMuPDF and vision-enabled LLMs (specifically gpt-4o) to parse text and UML diagrams. To ensure educational reliability and mitigate hallucinations, CAPRA introduces a deterministic Evidence Anchoring step using fuzzy matching via normalized Levenshtein distance, along with a ConsistencyManager agent that cross-verifies, deduplicates, and merges findings. System performance is assessed using a structured eight-criterion binary evaluation taxonomy covering: (i) extraction completeness, (ii) feature validation, (iii) issue grounding and severity detection, (iv) recommendation specificity and traceability, and (v) template and tone compliance. A preliminary empirical evaluation on 10 student reports shows that CAPRA satisfied 88.8% of the evaluated criteria under a strict two-rater aggregation rule, achieved moderate inter-rater agreement with human evaluators (kappa = 0.582), and processed each report in slightly over 4 minutes. While these results support the viability of LLM-supported architectural feedback, human oversight remains essential for subjective assessment dimensions.

AI.tldr: CAPRA is a system that uses multiple specialized large language model agents to provide automated technical feedback on software architecture deliverables. It achieved nearly 89% satisfaction of evaluation criteria and moderate agreement with human reviewers, but still requires human oversight.
AI.motivation: Automating assessment in software architecture education poses challenges due to the structural and traceability requirements of deliverables. The motivation is to address this gap using advanced large language model technologies.
AI.method: CAPRA employs a multi-agent system integrating PyMuPDF, vision-enabled LLMs, and fuzzy matching techniques to extract and analyze text and diagrams. It ensures reliability through a deterministic Evidence Anchoring step and a ConsistencyManager for validating findings.
AI.result: CAPRA demonstrated the ability to meet 88.8% of evaluation criteria with moderate agreement to human reviewers while processing each report in just over 4 minutes.
AI.conclusion: CAPRA is a promising system for automating feedback on architecture deliverables, though human oversight remains necessary for subjective evaluations.

title: Montreal Forced Aligner and the state of speech-to-text alignment in 2026
summary: The Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) was released in 2016 and has since become the most widely used tool for forced alignment in research and industry. In the decade since, MFA has undergone substantial development, including expanded coverage across more languages and dialects using larger open-source datasets, harmonized IPA dictionaries, model adaptation, cross-language phone remapping, and support utilities. This paper documents MFA 3.0's developments since version 1.0 and evaluates MFA's performance across English, Japanese, and Korean, benchmarked against classic and neural forced aligners. MFA 3.0 achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art performance across all four benchmark datasets with mean boundary errors below 15 ms. Adaptation and cross-language remapping are effective for languages outside MFA's training distribution, and pronunciation probability modeling and phonological rules provide gains in specific conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the major updates and performance of the Montreal Forced Aligner (MFA) tool, including its expanded functionalities and evaluation in forced alignment tasks.
AI.motivation: To document the advancements and performance improvements in the MFA tool, aiming to enhance its utility in linguistic research and industry applications.
AI.method: The paper details MFA 3.0's features like expanded language coverage, model adaptation, and pronunciation probability modeling, and benchmarks its performance against other aligners across several datasets.
AI.result: MFA 3.0 demonstrates state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art performance with mean boundary errors under 15 ms across English, Japanese, and Korean alignment tasks.
AI.conclusion: The advancements in MFA 3.0, such as cross-language remapping and phonological modeling, improve its effectiveness for a wide range of languages and foster its continued use in academic and industrial contexts.

title: Benchmarking Action Spaces in Reinforcement Learning for Vision-based Robotic Manipulation
summary: In real-world reinforcement learning (RL), the choice of action space can play a key role in shaping motion smoothness, safety, and overall task performance. In this study, we evaluate pose increment, pose velocity, joint position increment, and joint velocity across two vision-based manipulation tasks: object picking and pushing. We train policies in simulation and deploy them to the real world using sim-to-real transfer. We find that action-space representation indeed significantly affects sim-to-real performance. In particular, we find that the joint velocity action space is best for the vision-based picking and pushing tasks in terms of smoothness and final task performance. We also provide practical guidance for RL practitioners in choosing action spaces for both simulation and real-world experiments.

AI.tldr: Action-space choice influences real-world RL performance; joint velocity outperforms others in vision-based tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand how action-space choice impacts motion smoothness, safety, and task performance in RL.
AI.method: Evaluate four action-space types across two vision-based manipulation tasks, using sim-to-real transfer methodologies.
AI.result: Joint velocity action space achieved the best results for smoothness and task performance in picking and pushing tasks.
AI.conclusion: Action-space representation greatly affects RL outcomes, with joint velocity being the most effective for certain tasks; practical guidance is provided for RL practitioners.

title: Architectural Bias in Face Presentation Attack Detection: A Comparative Study of Vision Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks
summary: Face Presentation Attack Detection (PAD) systems constitute a critical security layer in biometric authentication; however, existing approaches exhibit systematic performance disparities across demographic groups, disproportionately affecting individuals with darker skin tones. This paper presents a comparative empirical investigation of whether Vision Transformer architectures reduce demographic bias in face PAD systems relative to convolutional baselines. Experiments are conducted on the CASIA-SURF Cross-Ethnicity Face Anti-Spoofing (CeFA) dataset. Three architectures are evaluated: a Multimodal ViT-Tiny trained from scratch, a ResNet18 CNN baseline, and a pretrained DeiT-S fine-tuned on CeFA across African, East Asian, and zero-shot Central Asian demographic groups. DeiT-S achieves the highest overall accuracy of 97.27% and the lowest EER of 0.86%, outperforming ResNet18 at 90.15% accuracy. In terms of fairness, DeiT-S reduces the inter-ethnic ACER gap between African and East Asian subjects to 0.13%, compared to 0.75% reported in an LBP-based work [6], representing an 83% reduction. Most notably, while ResNet18 records a BPCER of 10.44% on zero-shot Central Asian subjects, DeiT-S maintains 2.89% on the same unseen group, demonstrating a 3.6x generalization advantage. These results suggest that pretrained Vision Transformers achieve superior PAD accuracy, produce smaller demographic performance gaps, and generalize more equitably across unseen demographic groups, indicating that cross-demographic fairness in PAD may partly be influenced by architectural design.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates if Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures reduce demographic bias in Face PAD systems, finding that they outperform convolutional baselines in accuracy, fairness, and generalization.
AI.motivation: Address systematic demographic performance disparities in Face PAD systems, focusing on reducing bias against darker skin tones.
AI.method: Conduct empirical tests on the CeFA dataset using Multimodal ViT-Tiny, ResNet18, and pretrained DeiT-S architectures to assess accuracy, fairness, and demographic performance gaps.
AI.result: DeiT-S achieved top performance with 97.27% accuracy, minimizing demographic bias and exhibiting superior generalization (e.g., a 3.6x improvement for zero-shot Central Asian subjects).
AI.conclusion: Pretrained Vision Transformers, like DeiT-S, enhance demographic fairness, accuracy, and generalization in Face PAD systems. Architectural design can influence fairness.

title: FOSC-X: An Extended Framework for Optimal Local Cuts and Non-Horizontal Cluster Selection from Clustering Hierarchies
summary: Extracting a flat clustering solution from a hierarchy is a common task in practical cluster analysis and can be formulated as an optimisation problem. Existing approaches focus on finding a single optimal solution. We introduce FOSC-X, a framework for extracting the top-M globally optimal flat clusterings from local, non-horizontal cuts of a hierarchical cluster tree, while optionally enforcing constraints on the number of clusters. This enables automatic identification of multiple high-quality alternative clusterings that capture different aspects of the hierarchical structure. Without constraints, the top-M problem can be solved in polynomial time using dynamic programming, exploiting the property that locally optimal partial candidates within subtrees can be combined to form globally optimal solutions while automatically determining the number of clusters. However, this can lead to solutions with numbers of clusters that are ultimately undesirable -- e.g., too large to be meaningful or practically analysed within a particular application domain. Imposing cluster-count constraints breaks the optimality property underlying the unconstrained dynamic programming approach, since locally optimal partial candidates may no longer combine into feasible globally optimal solutions. FOSC-X addresses this challenge through a dynamic programming strategy that maintains compact sets of feasible candidates using lower and upper feasibility bounds while pruning infeasible or dominated combinations. The resulting method guarantees optimal rankings of the top-M solutions with linear-time complexity in the number of cluster nodes and dataset size, both with and without cluster-count constraints. Experiments show that FOSC-X efficiently reveals alternative clustering structures overlooked by single-solution extraction methods.

AI.tldr: FOSC-X is a framework that extracts the top-M optimal flat clusterings from hierarchical cluster trees, including optional constraints on cluster counts, enabling efficient identification of alternative high-quality solutions.
AI.motivation: Existing clustering methods typically focus on identifying a single optimal solution, which may overlook alternative, high-quality clustering solutions that better capture various aspects of data structure. There is a need for a method that efficiently identifies multiple optimal clusterings with optional constraints on cluster counts.
AI.method: FOSC-X solves the top-M optimal flat clustering problem using dynamic programming. It ensures polynomial time complexity by combining locally optimal partial solutions into globally optimal ones. For cases with cluster-count constraints, it employs a modified strategy with feasibility bounds and pruning to maintain optimal rankings efficiently.
AI.result: The framework guarantees optimal rankings of the top-M clusterings with linear-time complexity in terms of cluster tree nodes and dataset size. It is efficient in identifying alternative clustering structures that single-solution methods miss.
AI.conclusion: FOSC-X provides an efficient and flexible solution to derive multiple high-quality flat clusterings, accommodating scenarios both with and without constraints on cluster counts. It unlocks alternative perspectives on hierarchical data structures while maintaining computational efficiency.

title: TRIDENT: Breaking the Hybrid-Safety-Physics Coupling for Provably Safe Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Safe coordination in networked cyber-physical systems forces learning algorithms to simultaneously handle hybrid discrete-continuous actions, hard training-time safety constraints, and physics-governed dynamics. We show that these three features form a directed cycle of biases that defeats any naive composition of off-the-shelf modules, and formalize this as a three-way coupling lemma. We then introduce TRIDENT, the first MARL framework whose three components are co-designed to cancel each leak: a Richardson-Romberg gradient correction reducing Gumbel-Softmax bias from O(tau) to O(tau^2), a Lyapunov-constrained sequential trust-region update enforcing per-iterate feasibility, and a physics-informed residual critic that decomposes value rather than reward. We prove an O~(1/sqrt(K)) convergence rate to a constrained Nash equilibrium and an O(sqrt(K)) cumulative-violation bound. On multi-UAV mobile-edge computing, autonomous intersection management, and a hybrid SMAC variant, TRIDENT cuts training-time violations by 95.5% over MADDPG and 76.3% over MACPO, while improving reward by 13.5% over the strongest unconstrained baseline.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the TRIDENT framework for safe coordination in networked cyber-physical systems, achieving substantial safety and performance improvements.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges posed by hybrid actions, training-time safety constraints, and physics dynamics in learning algorithms, and their inherent interdependence.
AI.method: The authors present TRIDENT, a framework combining a Richardson-Romberg gradient correction, Lyapunov-constrained trust-region update, and physics-informed residual critic.
AI.result: TRIDENT achieves a 95.5% reduction in training-time safety violations over MADDPG, 76.3% over MACPO, and improves rewards by 13.5% over the strongest unconstrained baseline.
AI.conclusion: TRIDENT successfully integrates its components to overcome the biases in learning for cyber-physical systems, setting a new benchmark for safety and efficiency.

title: Compressed-Resident Genomics: Full-Pipeline Device-Resident GPU LZ77 Decode with Position-Invariant Random Access
comment: 5 pages, 3 tables. ACEAPEX and aceapex_cuda included in lzbench 2.3
summary: Genomic archives grow faster than decompression keeps up: the European Nucleotide Archive holds tens of petabytes of fastq.gz, and gzip is fundamentally sequential. GPU decompressors (nvCOMP DEFLATE at ~50GB/s on A100) decode whole files with no random access; CPU genomic tools (CRAM, samtools) support region seeks but only at CPU speed. We extend ACEAPEX, an absolute-offset parallel LZ77 codec included in the official lzbench 2.3 release, with three contributions absent from our prior work. First, a full device-resident GPU decode pipeline (entropy and match resolution both on-device) reaching up to 260GB/s on FASTQ, closing the match-phase-only gap of the earlier paper. Second, position-invariant random access with a compact coordinate index: an arbitrary read decodes in 0.362ms, ~6x faster than warm samtools faidx, with a read-to-block index 6.3x smaller than a .fai. Third, a range-decode strategy that decouples output size from VRAM, sustaining 165.7GB/s on a 50GB genome where whole-file decode runs out of memory. All results are bit-perfect. We also measure Meta's open DietGPU ANS on H100 at 592GB/s decode, faster than the proprietary entropy stage we currently use, showing a fully open high-throughput stack is viable. Code is MIT-licensed.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a GPU-based genomic decompression method that achieves superior speed and efficiency compared to traditional CPU methods, including position-invariant random access and VRAM-friendly strategies.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth of genomic archives outpaces the ability of existing decompression tools, creating a need for faster decompression solutions that also enable efficient random access for genomic data.
AI.method: The authors extend the ACEAPEX codec by adding a GPU-resident decode pipeline, implementing a compact coordinate indexing for random access, and developing a range-decode strategy to handle memory limitations.
AI.result: Their approach achieves up to 260GB/s for decoding FASTQ files, 0.362ms for arbitrary read access, and efficient decoding of large genomes without running out of memory, offering performance significantly better than traditional tools like samtools.
AI.conclusion: The presented methods significantly enhance the speed and efficiency of genomic data decompression and random access, making it feasible to keep up with the pace of genomic database growth. Furthermore, the open MIT license ensures accessibility for broad adoption and integration.

title: ProfiLLM: Utility-Aligned Agentic User Profiling for Industrial Ride-Hailing Dispatch
summary: Bringing Large Language Models (LLMs) into industrial ride-hailing dispatch as semantic feature extractors over platform-scale behavioral logs is a compelling but under-explored data systems problem. Production matching pipelines remain dominated by structured numerical features, yet decisive behavioral signals (e.g., a driver's habitual aversion to certain regions) are inherently contextual and naturally expressible as LLM-generated user profiles. However, scaling such profiling to a live, millisecond-latency dispatcher faces three intertwined constraints rarely addressed together: on a platform with millions of daily orders, logs exceed any LLM's context window by orders of magnitude; most users are long-tail, with too few interactions for per-user profiling; and surface-fluent profiles do not necessarily improve downstream prediction utility. We present ProfiLLM, an agentic LLM data pipeline that operationalizes utility-aligned user profiling for production matching systems through two modules. (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining equips an LLM agent with 27 analytical tools to mine platform-scale data, producing reusable global knowledge, adaptive user clustering rules, and region-level supply-demand priors. (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration generates multiple candidate profiles per cluster, evaluates them via a lightweight downstream utility proxy, iteratively refines the best candidates and constructs preference pairs for DPO fine-tuning. Deployed on DiDi's production dispatcher, ProfiLLM achieves up to +6.14% relative AUC improvement in outcome prediction, up to +4.35% GMV gain in dispatching simulation, and consistent improvements in a 14-day online A/B test including +0.47% GMV, +0.33% Completion Rate, and -0.82% Cancel-Before-Accept rate.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces ProfiLLM, a system using Large Language Models (LLMs) for crafting utility-driven user profiles to enhance ride-hailing dispatch systems, yielding noticeable improvements in prediction accuracy and dispatch outcomes.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the limitations of conventional structured numerical features in ride-hailing dispatch systems. It seeks to leverage contextual behavioral signals that LLMs can extract, such as user preferences, to close the gap between large-scale behavioral data and actionable production systems.
AI.method: ProfiLLM employs a two-module system: (1) Tool-Augmented Global Knowledge Mining, using LLM agents with analytical tools to extract global trends, user clustering rules, and supply-demand patterns; (2) Utility-Aligned Profile Exploration, iteratively refining profiles to align with business goals using a downstream utility proxy and fine-tuning with preference data.
AI.result: The proposed system, when implemented on DiDi's live dispatcher, showed significant performance gains, including up to +6.14% in prediction AUC, +4.35% GMV gain in simulation, and strong real-world online A/B test improvements such as +0.47% GMV and -0.82% cancellation rate.
AI.conclusion: ProfiLLM proves that LLMs can successfully scale to production systems to create context-aware user profiles that improve industrial ride-hailing dispatch metrics. This study provides a blueprint for integrating semantic profiling into platforms with massive-scale behavioral data.

title: Where Did the Variability Go? From Vibe Coding to Product Lines by Regeneration
summary: In vibe coding, an emerging AI-driven paradigm, an LLM generates an entire program from a natural language prompt, but what happens to the variability that traditional software engineering carefully builds into code? To answer this question, we conducted an exploratory analysis on 10 vibe coded C/C++ projects, which suggests that there is near-zero in-artifact variability, i.e., at compile and runtime. All variability decisions are resolved at a single new binding time, generation time, the moment the LLM produces the source code. Rather than treating this as a defect to fix, we propose Variability by Regeneration (VbR), to our knowledge the first product-line approach in which the LLM acts as the derivation engine, generating a purpose-built, free of dead code binary for each variant from a declarative specification, while a variant dispatcher transparently routes user requests to the matching binary. We formalise VbR, contrast it with classical SPL derivation, and demonstrate its full pipeline on a wc product family. For SPL engineering, variability in AI-generated software belongs in the specification, not in the code.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Variability by Regeneration (VbR), an approach where an LLM generates software variants on demand, eliminating in-code variability and formalizing this methodology.
AI.motivation: To address how traditional variability mechanisms in software systems can be reimagined in the context of AI-driven code generation by Large Language Models (LLMs).
AI.method: Analyzed 10 C/C++ projects created via vibe coding, proposing a new method (VbR) where variant generation is performed at generation time by leveraging LLMs, supported by a formalized framework and validated through an example pipeline of a word count (wc) product family.
AI.result: Near-zero in-code variability was observed; all variability decisions shifted to generation time. The Variability by Regeneration pipeline was successfully demonstrated.
AI.conclusion: Variability for AI-generated software should reside in high-level specifications, with LLMs dynamically generating targeted variants. This approach differs from classical software product line methods by avoiding in-code variability.

title: Possible or Definite? A Benchmark for Evaluating Diagnostic Uncertainty Preservation in Clinical Text
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for clinical text tasks such as summarization and revision. While most studies evaluate the fluency and coherence of LLM-generated text, whether LLMs correctly preserve diagnostic uncertainty remains underexplored. In clinical practice, phrases such as ``possible pneumonia'' communicate the strength of available evidence and directly guide decisions about follow-up testing and treatment. Altering these uncertainty expressions can change the clinical meaning entirely. In this paper, we systematically evaluated this problem in two steps. First, we constructed a benchmark of 1,200 clinical documents with 9,184 uncertainty annotations across five levels. Second, we evaluated three LLMs on this benchmark. Our results show that (1) LLMs preserve the original uncertainty cues poorly, often less than half the time; (2) LLMs struggle with nuanced distinctions between adjacent levels. This work reveals a failure mode not captured by standard evaluation metrics and provides implications for the safe deployment of LLMs in clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models (LLMs) preserve diagnostic uncertainty in clinical text tasks and finds that they perform poorly, especially in nuanced distinctions.
AI.motivation: To assess whether LLMs can correctly maintain diagnostic uncertainty, which is crucial for accurate clinical decision-making and often neglected in standard evaluations of LLM performance.
AI.method: The authors created a benchmark of 1,200 clinical documents with 9,184 uncertainty annotations across five levels and used it to evaluate the performance of three LLMs in preserving these uncertainty cues.
AI.result: LLMs poorly preserved diagnostic uncertainty cues, often failing over 50% of the time, and showed particular difficulty in distinguishing nuanced adjacent levels of uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit significant weaknesses in handling diagnostic uncertainty, revealing risks in their deployment for clinical applications unless these limitations are addressed.

title: Admittance-Based Surface Alignment for Human-in-the-Loop Robotic Visual Inspection
summary: Precision visual inspection underpins quality assurance across aerospace, semiconductor, and medical manufacturing, where undetected surface anomalies on high-value parts translate directly into scrap, rework, and field failures. Robotic visual inspection requires precise alignment between the end-effector and local surface geometry in the presence of perception noise and surface irregularities. In industrial settings, a human operator is often kept in the loop via teleoperation or shared autonomy, introducing real-time adjustments that render purely offline motion planning inadequate. This motivates control architectures capable of reactive, compliant behavior under combined human and perceptual uncertainty. This paper presents a novel real-time, closed-loop robotic orientation control pipeline for precision visual inspection, with an admittance-based framework that unifies operator input and perception-driven surface alignment. We design the end-effector as a virtual sphere moving through a viscous medium, such that the resulting physically interpretable mass--damper system generates synchronized, compliant motion from orientation error and operator commands. We validate the framework on a 6-DOF manipulator demonstrating stable normal-tracking and a final mean orientation error of 0.4°.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a real-time, closed-loop robotic orientation control system for precision visual inspection, combining operator input with perception-driven alignment to minimize errors in industrial settings.
AI.motivation: The aim is to address challenges in robotic visual inspection where precise alignment is hindered by perception noise, surface irregularities, and the need for real-time human input in industrial applications.
AI.method: The authors propose an admittance-based framework that treats the end-effector as a virtual sphere moving through a viscous medium, generating compliant motion through a mass-damper system. This integrates human operator input and sensor feedback.
AI.result: The system was validated using a 6-DOF manipulator, achieving stable normal-tracking and a mean orientation error of 0.4°.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework successfully provides reactive, compliant behaviors for robotic visual inspection, improving alignment accuracy under human and perceptual uncertainties.

title: A Prototypical Signature Approach for Writer-Independent Offline Signature Verification
comment: Accepted for oral presentation at the International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR) 2026
summary: Offline handwritten signature verification aims to distinguish genuine from forged signatures using static images. Since real forgeries are rarely available, negative samples are usually randomly drawn from genuine signatures of other users to create training data. However, this random selection often lacks diversity, increases redundancy, and escalates computational cost, leading to inefficient training. We propose a data-driven strategy to generate diverse, informative negative samples using prototypical signatures, which are compact, non-identifiable summaries of genuine signature features. Based on the experiments results, we conclude that (i) prototypical signatures yield more informative negative samples, improving the detection of skilled forgeries; (ii) the proposed approach is backbone-agnostic, showing robustness across architectures; and (iii) when combined with a primal-form linear SVM, it serves as an alternative to RBF-based models while significantly improving scalability and computational efficiency. Implementation of the method is available at https://github.com/kdmoura/proto_hsv.

AI.tldr: The study proposes using prototypical signatures to create diverse, efficient negative samples for offline handwritten signature verification, improving detection and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Improving the detection of genuine and forged signatures is critical, but current methods create training data with redundant, less diverse negative samples, leading to inefficient training.
AI.method: The paper introduces a data-driven strategy to generate informative negative samples using prototypical signatures, which summarize genuine signature features compactly. The approach is tested across architectures and with a primal-form linear SVM.
AI.result: The proposed strategy yields better detection of skilled forgeries, shows robustness across different architectures, and enhances scalability and efficiency when compared to traditional RBF-based methods.
AI.conclusion: Prototypical signatures improve detection of forged signatures, offer a backbone-agnostic approach, and significantly reduce computational costs. The implementation is open-source for broader accessibility.

title: Sequential Kernel-based Conditional Independence Testing via Adaptive Betting
summary: Testing conditional independence is fundamental yet intrinsically difficult: without additional assumptions, Type I error control is impossible in general. The "Model-X'' paradigm addresses this difficulty by assuming exact knowledge of a relevant conditional distribution. While small deviations from this assumption can sometimes be tolerated in classical one-shot testing, existing sequential conditional independence tests typically require the Model-X conditional to be known exactly, making them fragile when it must instead be estimated. We propose a new approach that is substantially more robust to such estimation error. Our method applies testing-by-betting to an adaptively optimized Kernel Conditional Independence statistic, together with a normalization scheme and a truncate-and-shift calibration strategy. These modifications greatly reduce Type I error inflation while preserving high power across high-dimensional synthetic benchmarks and real-world fairness tasks, outperforming existing sequential Model-X approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/he-zh/SKCI.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a more robust sequential conditional independence testing method that reduces error inflation and maintains high power by leveraging kernel statistics and betting-based adaptation.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the fragility of existing sequential conditional independence tests which struggle with errors when the "Model-X" conditional distribution is only estimated rather than known exactly.
AI.method: A normalization scheme alongside a truncate-and-shift calibration strategy is combined with adaptively optimized kernel conditional independence statistics, using the testing-by-betting framework.
AI.result: Their method significantly reduces Type I error inflation and performs well across high-dimensional synthetic benchmarks and real-world fairness tasks, outperforming prior Model-X techniques.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a more robust tool for conditional independence testing when handling estimated conditional distributions, with demonstrated efficacy in both synthetic and practical scenarios.

title: SAGE: Retain-Aware Post-Hoc Sanitization of Final Unlearning Vector
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning aims to remove undesirable knowledge or behaviors while preserving retained capabilities. Current unlearning methods all involve a trade-off between unlearning and retention. We have found that the retention activation bias can also be used to quantify the damage an unlearning method inflicts on retention, without considering the specific implementation of the unlearning process. This allows us to restore retention performance for any unlearning method using a post-hoc approach. Therefore, we propose a complementary post-hoc setting to sanitize the final update vector without rerunning the original unlearning pipeline. In this setting, we design SAGE, Spectral Activation-GEometry Sanitization, a source-agnostic correction for final unlearning updates. SAGE collects real module inputs from a small retain proxy, extracts their dominant activation geometry, and solves a source-anchored optimization objective in closed form, which suppresses update components aligned with high-energy retained directions while preserving the source method's forgetting carrier. Across multiple unlearning methods, model scales, and benchmarks, SAGE consistently relieves the retain-forget trade-off, identifying post-hoc sanitization of final vectors as a practical and underexplored axis for machine unlearning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SAGE, a post-hoc method to improve knowledge retention in Large Language Model (LLM) unlearning without affecting the unlearning process.
AI.motivation: Existing unlearning methods for large language models suffer from a trade-off between effectively forgetting targeted knowledge and retaining desirable knowledge and performance.
AI.method: The authors propose SAGE, a post-hoc method that sanitizes the final update vectors from any unlearning procedure by leveraging the dominant activation geometry of a retain proxy and solving an optimization problem to suppress retention damage.
AI.result: SAGE demonstrated consistent alleviation of the retain-forget trade-off across multiple unlearning methods, model scales, and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc sanitization of unlearning vectors, as performed by SAGE, is a practical and effective approach to enhancing knowledge retention in LLM unlearning.

title: Urban Limits as Design Constraints: Identifying Suitable Locations for Distributed, Photovoltaic-Powered Servers
comment: Paper in Proceedings of LIMITS 2026: 12th Workshop on Computing within Limits, 2026-06-23-25, Online
summary:
Urban territories face growing tensions between increasing digital demand, limited resources, and socially constrained built environments. Although distributed computing paradigms such as edge and fog computing are widely presented as solutions for reducing latency and energy dissipation, the scientific literature largely overlooks where such infrastructures can be physically and socially deployed within cities, and typically neglects urban constraints, environmental impacts, and equity considerations.
  This paper proposes a methodology for identifying suitable urban locations for deploying distributed servers under structural, environmental, and social limits. Relying exclusively on existing infrastructures and anthropised surfaces, it combines legal frameworks, ongoing urban projects, citizen consultations, and scientific literature to construct a place-based glossary of viable site typologies, evaluated through energy, spatial, and qualitative criteria. Applied to the French city of Montpellier, our results illustrate how urban constraints and local resources shape the feasibility of decentralised, solar-powered digital infrastructures, and highlight the value of territorialised approaches for rethinking digital services within urban limits.
