AI.tldr: This paper offers a methodology to deploy distributed computing servers in urban areas while factoring in structural, environmental, and social constraints.
AI.motivation: Urban areas face pressures from rising digital demand, constrained resources, and the limitations of built environments, necessitating context-specific strategies for infrastructure deployment.
AI.method: The study uses existing urban infrastructures and combines legal, social, and urban planning analyses to identify viable locations for distributed servers, considering energy, spatial, and qualitative criteria.
AI.result: Applying the method to Montpellier, France, demonstrates how urban limitations and local resources influence the feasibility of solar-powered, decentralized digital infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: Decentralized digital systems within cities require a place-based, territorial strategy that respects urban constraints and maximizes local resources.

title: WorldLines: Benchmarking and Modeling Long-Horizon Stateful Embodied Agents
summary: To assist humans over extended periods in real homes, embodied agents must remember user routines, world states, and past interactions. Existing long-term memory benchmarks mainly evaluate language-centric retrieval and question answering, while embodied benchmarks often focus on short-horizon task execution without testing long-term memory use in dynamic environments. We introduce WorldLines, a project-driven benchmark for long-horizon embodied household assistance. It constructs temporally extended household traces with dialogues, actions, execution feedback, object and device state changes, and converts them into evidence-linked samples for Memory QA and Embodied Task Planning. We further propose ObsMem, an observer-grounded memory framework that maintains visibility-aware memories and action-native state trails for state-aware decisions. Experiments reveal persistent challenges in partial observability, overwritten world states, and translating long-term memory into embodied plans, while ObsMem offers a stronger reference architecture for this setting.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WorldLines, a benchmark for long-horizon memory in embodied household assistance, and proposes ObsMem, a framework for improving memory usage in such scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks lack the ability to evaluate long-term memory use in dynamic, real-world embodied tasks, which is necessary for effective household assistance.
AI.method: The authors created WorldLines, a benchmark for assessing memory and task planning in household settings, and designed ObsMem, a memory framework that incorporates observer-grounded visibility and action-driven state tracking.
AI.result: WorldLines provides a new way to evaluate memory usage, and ObsMem addressed challenges like partial observability and state overwriting better than existing models.
AI.conclusion: WorldLines and ObsMem collectively advance the study of long-term memory in embodied agents, addressing key challenges and providing robust tools for future research.

title: Written by AI, Managed by AI: Semantic Space Control and Index Sickness Elimination Across 391 Consecutive Sessions
comment: 22 pages, 2 tables, 1 figure. Action research. Bilingual submission (Chinese companion version included as supplementary). Submitted to ICSE 2027 IOR track
summary: The prevailing engineering intuition for addressing conceptual drift in long-horizon LLM collaboration is to trade more formal constraints for more reliable outputs -- designing symbolic identifier systems, accumulating defensive rules in System Prompts, expanding context windows. Our engineering record shows that in long-horizon settings, this direction may produce effects contrary to design intent. Using action research methods in a real software project (Bang-v3) spanning approximately one month and 391 collaborative sessions, we document and analyze the failure process of these strategies. When the symbolic system exceeds a complexity threshold, LLMs do not become more accurate -- instead, they abandon genuine understanding of business semantics, retreat to self-referential reasoning within the symbolic layer, and generate outputs that appear internally consistent but are physically disconnected from reality. We name this failure pattern "Index Sickness," and its canonical manifestation "Phantom Legislation." We name the underlying principle the "Pang Principle (Semantic Vitality Law)": natural language carrying explicit purpose conveys far greater information quality than symbolic expression. From this, we design and validate its physical engineering mechanism: "Baseline-Log Physical Separation." In the same project, this mechanism reduced AI Instructions volume by ~75%, and across the subsequent ~150 sessions, no recurrence of Index Sickness was observed. A bilingual companion version (Chinese) is included as supplementary material.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses the counterproductive effects of imposing formal constraints on long-horizon LLM collaborations, identifies a failure pattern named 'Index Sickness,' and introduces a solution based on natural language vitality.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of conceptual drift in long-horizon collaborations with LLMs and improve reliability in software project outcomes.
AI.method: Action research in a real project spanning one month and 391 collaborative sessions, documenting and analyzing failures of symbolic systems and introducing the 'Baseline-Log Physical Separation' mechanism.
AI.result: The proposed mechanism reduced the volume of AI instructions by ~75% and prevented the recurrence of 'Index Sickness' in subsequent sessions.
AI.conclusion: Natural language with explicit purpose offers higher information quality than overly complex symbolic systems, and the introduced mechanism effectively mitigates failure patterns like 'Index Sickness.'

title: PreUnlearn: Auditing Collateral Knowledge Damage Before Large Language Model Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning for large language models (LLMs) aims to remove specified knowledge while preserving the rest of the model's capabilities. However, the boundary between knowledge to forget and knowledge to retain is often unclear, since related and even distant information may be entangled in the model. In this paper, we study LLM unlearning from a data-centric perspective and measure how unlearning effects propagate from the forget set to same-domain and distant-domain knowledge. We find a consistent decay pattern: collateral damage is strongest near the forget set, weakens with semantic distance, but does not disappear at domain boundaries. We further ask whether such damage can be audited before unlearning is executed. We formulate forget-set auditing as a pre-unlearning prediction task and analyze which data features are most predictive of downstream damage. Our results show that interaction features between the forget set and evaluation set provide the strongest signals, suggesting that collateral damage is partly reflected in data geometry before model updates occur. These findings position forget-set auditing as an early warning tool for identifying risky unlearning runs and designing more reliable unlearning procedures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies machine unlearning for LLMs, focusing on the propagation of unlearning effects and the possibility of auditing such effects before implementation.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of selectively removing knowledge from LLMs while preserving overall functionality, especially given the difficulty in distinguishing what should be forgotten from what must be retained.
AI.method: Investigated the propagation of unlearning effects through a data-centric lens and formulated a predictive task, forget-set auditing, to analyze data features that indicate potential damage before unlearning.
AI.result: Found a consistent pattern where unlearning causes collateral damage that diminishes with semantic distance but crosses domain boundaries. Identified that data interaction features are predictive of collateral damage.
AI.conclusion: Forget-set auditing presents a viable tool for assessing risks in unlearning processes and guiding the design of more reliable unlearning techniques.

title: SC3-Eval: Evaluating Robot Foundation Models via Self-Consistent Video Generation
summary: Evaluating generalist robot manipulation policies in the real world is expensive, slow, and difficult to scale. Action-conditioned video world models offer a scalable alternative by simulating policy rollouts. Autoregressive rollouts accumulate compounding errors, observations across multiple camera views must remain mutually consistent, and the evaluator must generalize to policies whose behaviors lie outside the training distribution. We address these challenges with SC3-Eval, a self-consistent video generation recipe that adapts a pre-trained video foundation model into an accurate policy evaluator by enforcing three complementary forms of consistency. First, forward-inverse dynamics consistency jointly trains the model to predict frames from actions and to recover actions from frames, anchoring generated rollouts to a physically plausible action manifold and counteracting the drift a forward-only model cannot penalize. Second, cross-view consistency trains the model to inpaint each camera view from the other, keeping the multi-camera observation coherent over long rollouts without any explicit memory mechanism. Third, test-time consistency reuses the inverse dynamics mode at inference as a per-action-chunk uncertainty signal that terminates rollouts whose generated frames drift away from the requested actions. We also demonstrate SC3-Eval rollouts reproduce the failure modes that policies exhibit in real-world rollouts, supporting fine-grained diagnostic comparison rather than aggregate ranking alone. Across seven real-world vision-language-action policies, SC3-Eval attains a closed-loop Pearson correlation of $0.929$ and MMRV of $0.119$, outperforming three strong prior video-model-based baselines, and generalizes to new tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SC3-Eval, a method to adapt pre-trained video world models for evaluating robotic manipulation policies through self-consistent video generation.
AI.motivation: Real-world robotic policy evaluation is costly and difficult, and existing video world models struggle with issues like compounding errors and maintaining consistency across multi-camera setups.
AI.method: SC3-Eval enforces three forms of consistency: forward-inverse dynamics (ensures physical plausibility), cross-view (maintains consistency between multi-camera views), and test-time (uses uncertainty to terminate unrealistic rollouts).
AI.result: SC3-Eval shows high correlation with real-world outcomes ($0.929$ Pearson correlation) and outperforms prior baselines while reproducing realistic failure modes in simulated rollouts.
AI.conclusion: SC3-Eval offers a scalable, accurate tool for evaluating robotic policies, even handling unseen tasks, making it a practical alternative to expensive real-world testing.

title: Hierarchical Multi-Modal Retrieval for Knowledge-Grounded News Image Captioning
summary: Traditional image captioning methods often struggle to generate comprehensive, context-rich descriptions, especially for details not directly observable from visual cues. To overcome this, we propose a novel retrieval-augmented image captioning framework that generates captions with deeper insights, such as object attributes, event context, and underlying significance, by leveraging external knowledge. Our approach features a hierarchical multi-modal article retrieval mechanism that moves beyond monolithic text entities. This retrieval considers article structure-aware features, including weighted textual components (e.g., headlines, body sections) and visual placement patterns, alongside multi-faceted similarity computations (content--visual, visual--visual, and discourse positioning). A subsequent contextual relevance refinement stage further enhances the retrieved information. The retrieved articles then serve as the knowledge base for caption generation: first, a VLM generates a concise image description; second, we segment relevant information from the retrieved articles based on this description; and finally, an LLM utilizes both the description and extracted knowledge to generate a comprehensive, contextually detailed caption. We participated in the ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge and achieved 5th place with an overall score of 0.2824 on the private test set of the OpenEvent-V1 dataset. Source code is publicly released at https://github.com/mf0212/EVENTA-Challange.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a retrieval-augmented image captioning framework that generates detailed captions by incorporating external knowledge.
AI.motivation: Traditional image captioning methods struggle with generating rich, context-aware descriptions, especially for details not visible in images. The paper aims to address this limitation by leveraging external information.
AI.method: The proposed framework retrieves structured articles using a hierarchical multi-modal approach based on textual, visual, and discourse features. Captions are then generated by combining VLM image descriptions with article-derived insights through an LLM.
AI.result: The approach achieved 5th place in the ACM Multimedia EVENTA 2025 Challenge with a score of 0.2824 on the private test set of the OpenEvent-V1 dataset.
AI.conclusion: Combining visual understanding with external knowledge can enhance image captioning, enabling more context-rich, descriptive outputs. The method balances precision and depth effectively.

title: Quantifying and Auditing LLM Evaluation via Positive--Unlabeled Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used as judges for scalable evaluation, yet such LLM--as--a--Judge systems exhibit systematic biases that are decoupled from semantic quality, most notably verbosity bias. Meanwhile, human supervision is costly and typically selective, yielding reliable positive judgments but leaving most outputs unlabelled and potentially mixed in quality. We formulate LLM evaluation under selective human supervision as a positive--unlabelled learning problem and propose a geometric auditing framework based on Partial Optimal Transport. By aligning a small set of human--verified positives with a reliable subset of unlabelled outputs in a fixed embedding space, our method identifies human--consistent preferences and corrects biased judges without retraining. Experiments demonstrate improved alignment with human preferences, increased robustness to presentation biases, and interpretable confidence estimates, offering a scalable and statistically grounded alternative to existing LLM--as--a--judge pipelines.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses biases in the use of Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges by proposing a geometric auditing method using Partial Optimal Transport, improving alignment with human preferences.
AI.motivation: Human supervision for evaluating LLMs is costly and limited, while LLM evaluations show systematic biases, such as verbosity bias. The work aims to address these issues for scalable and accurate evaluation.
AI.method: The authors frame the problem as positive-unlabelled learning and utilize a geometric auditing framework based on Partial Optimal Transport to align human-verified positives with reliable subsets of unlabelled outputs in a fixed embedding space.
AI.result: Experiments show that the method improves alignment with human preferences, increases robustness to biases, and provides interpretable confidence estimates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a scalable, statistically-sound alternative for LLM evaluation pipelines, mitigating biases without the need for retraining.

title: Ghost Attractor Networks: Basin-Structured Dynamical Decoders for Closed-Loop Sequential Generation
summary: Sequential output generation with large-scale Transformer and diffusion decoders pays a memory cost that grows with sequence length, plus iterative per-step computation. Replacing them with small feed-forward decoders restores efficiency but produces unstructured latent representations that limit closed-loop control: phase-conditioned action generation and cross-step latent carry-over both require a latent geometry with stable basins. This article proposes Ghost Attractor Networks, a theoretically derived dynamical decoder whose latent evolves under a learned potential with drift and produces a basin-attractor structure by construction. Three desiderata (multi-modality, decoder-level single-pass switching, and constant memory) motivate the potential-drift form, and mode transitions arise as saddle-node bifurcations with ghost-attractor escape. A hierarchical phase-space decomposition separates first-order basin convergence from second-order proprioceptive refinement. Empirically, a Ghost trained end-to-end with a behavioral-cloning and contrastive objective exhibits the predicted gradient-flow contraction in its potential, with the gradient norm decaying by 67 percent across five integration steps on 1430 held-out samples. Ghost is evaluated as a robotic action decoder. A 2.3-million-parameter Ghost matches the offline accuracy of a 1.07-billion-parameter Diffusion Transformer at 462 times fewer parameters and 32 times lower latency, and beats five alternative 2M-parameter decoders (MLP, Neural ODE, CVAE, Transformer, 1-step Diffusion) on offline mean squared error by 5.9 to 29 percent. On the LIBERO-10 closed-loop benchmark, phase conditioning on Ghost's basin-structured latent yields a 13.5 percentage-point success-rate gain over a feed-forward MLP baseline, and persistent-latent ensembling reaches a 95.7 percent final success rate.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Ghost Attractor Networks, a novel memory-efficient dynamical decoder providing structured latent spaces for tasks like robotic action generation, significantly outperforming existing models with lower computational costs.
AI.motivation: Traditional decoders, including Transformer and diffusion-based models, have high memory and computation costs for sequential generation, while smaller feed-forward decoders lack structured latent spaces necessary for complex control tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose a Ghost Attractor Network which integrates a learned potential with a dynamical latent evolution characterized by basin-attractor structures. The hierarchical phase-space decomposition enables robust convergence and refinements, with training including behavioral-cloning and contrastive objectives.
AI.result: Ghost achieves competitive or superior performance at a fraction of the computational resources. It matched the performance of a large Diffusion Transformer at 462 times fewer parameters and 32 times lower latency, and outperformed similar-sized alternative decoders on multiple evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging a theoretically-grounded approach to structured latent representations, Ghost Attractor Networks demonstrate promising gains in efficiency and accuracy for sequential generation tasks, particularly in robotics applications.

title: LiveStack: OS Support for Cluster-Scale Full-Stack Live Simulation
summary: Cluster-scale full-stack simulation is essential for evaluating distributed software stacks and emerging hardware components before deployment. Such simulation must achieve both full-stack fidelity for the unmodified production stack and the simulation performance required for iterative configuration exploration. However, no existing method achieves both. We present LiveStack, an OS-level approach to cluster-scale full-stack simulation built on top of the Linux virtualization stack. LiveStack comprises four subsystems: simulation-oriented scheduling, live memory hierarchy management, simulation-aware IPC, and distributed simulation orchestration. Together, they coordinate live and modeled components under shared simulated time while controlling interference among co-located live hosts. These mechanisms point toward simulation-native OS support, where simulation control and orchestration become core OS responsibilities.

AI.tldr: Cluster-scale full-stack simulation tool, LiveStack, is introduced for comprehensive software and hardware evaluation.
AI.motivation: There is a need for effective cluster-scale full-stack simulation to evaluate distributed software stacks and emerging hardware with high fidelity and performance.
AI.method: LiveStack uses an OS-level approach with four key subsystems: simulation-oriented scheduling, live memory hierarchy management, simulation-aware IPC, and distributed simulation orchestration.
AI.result: LiveStack enables coordinated simulation of live and modeled components, while reducing interference and maintaining simulated time fidelity.
AI.conclusion: LiveStack demonstrates the potential for future OS designs to natively support simulation control and orchestration.

title: Externalizing Research Synthesis and Validation in AI Scientists through a Research Harness
summary: AI systems can increasingly automate scientific workflows, but the reasoning that links prior evidence, generated ideas, experiments and final claims often remains implicit inside model inference. Here we introduce Xcientist, a research harness that externalizes research synthesis and experimental validation into inspectable, contract-governed processes. Xcientist organizes literature evidence, idea states, implementation plans, ablation records and repair traces as persistent research artifacts, so that generated mechanisms can be grounded, executed, tested and revised without losing their evidential basis. We identify claim drift as a failure mode of automated research, where runnable artifacts no longer support the mechanism originally claimed. Across training-free memory systems, graph-structured traffic forecasting and multi-scale physics-informed neural networks, Xcientist preserves traceable trajectories from problem formulation to mechanism design, validation and bounded revision. These results suggest that AI scientists should be evaluated not only by their final artifacts, but by whether their synthesis and validation processes remain attributable, inspectable and scientifically accountable.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Xcientist, a system that externalizes and organizes key steps in automated research to ensure transparency and traceability.
AI.motivation: AI systems often fail to make their reasoning in the research process explicit, leading to challenges in verifying claims and scientific accountability.
AI.method: Introduces Xcientist, which externalizes the synthesis and validation steps of scientific workflows into persistent, inspectable research artifacts subject to contract-governed processes.
AI.result: Xcientist preserves traceable processes from problem formulation to validation, identifying and addressing claim drift in automated research applications such as memory systems and neural networks.
AI.conclusion: The study suggests AI scientists should not only produce final outcomes, but also maintain traceable and scientifically accountable processes throughout their workflows.

title: Teaching Software Engineering with LLM and MCP Integration: From Classroom to Industry Practice
comment: Aceept by International Symposium on Educational Technology (ISET) 2026
summary: The rapid integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the Model Context Protocol (MCP) into industrial software engineering has created a pressing need to update software engineering education to align with emerging technologies and evolving industry demands. This study investigates an innovative approach that integrates LLMs and MCP into a collaborative teaching model for software engineering education, aiming to build a practical learning framework closely connected to real-world engineering practices. By embedding LLM and MCP driven tools into daily teaching, code assistance, and engineering simulations, the model effectively bridges the gap between traditional instruction and industrial workflows. This integration enhances students' programming competence, practical problem-solving abilities, and proficiency in using intelligent engineering tools. Furthermore, through partnerships with industry internships, students can apply these technologies in real-world settings, further strengthening the connection between academic preparation and professional practice. Overall, this research offers a practical pathway for reforming and innovating software engineering education in the era of artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) and Model Context Protocol (MCP) into software engineering education to modernize teaching and connect academics with industrial practices.
AI.motivation: There is a growing need for software engineering education to adapt to the use of LLMs and MCP in industry, addressing the gap between academic training and modern industrial workflows.
AI.method: The paper explores embedding LLM- and MCP-based tools into teaching activities, such as code assistance and engineering simulations, along with partnerships for industry internships.
AI.result: The proposed model enhances students' programming skills, problem-solving abilities, and expertise in intelligent engineering tools, while ensuring alignment with real-world workflows.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a framework for innovating software engineering education to meet the demands of the AI-driven era by integrating modern tools and aligning with industry practices.

title: Towards Scalable Customization and Deployment of Multi-Agent Systems for Enterprise Applications
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems demonstrate strong performance on complex reasoning and task execution, enabling broad enterprise applications. However, production deployment remains challenging due to domain-specific customization requirements and high latency and inference costs in agentic workflows. We propose a unified framework for customization and efficient deployment of multi-agent systems in real-world settings. The first stage, Agentic Model Customization, combines continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and preference optimization to adapt a compact model to specialized domains while retaining strong agentic capabilities. The second stage, Inference Optimization, integrates speculative decoding and FP8 quantization with targeted calibration to enable cost-efficient serving with minimal quality loss. Across enterprise workloads, our framework enables rapid domain adaptation and achieves a 4.48x speedup in throughput while maintaining performance and improving robustness on long-tail scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to customize and deploy LLM-based multi-agent systems with improved efficiency, focusing on reduced latency and cost.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems struggle with domain-specific customization needs and high operational costs, limiting their real-world applicability.
AI.method: The framework includes 'Agentic Model Customization' using methods like continual pretraining, supervised fine-tuning, and preference optimization, and 'Inference Optimization' employing speculative decoding and FP8 quantization with calibration.
AI.result: The proposed framework allows rapid domain adaptation and achieves a 4.48x speedup in throughput without compromising on performance or robustness, especially in long-tail scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The framework simplifies the deployment of LLM-based multi-agent systems in enterprise contexts, making them both cost-efficient and adaptable to specialized domains.

title: SRL: Combining SLIP Model and Reinforcement Learning for Agile Robotic Jumping
summary: Robotic jumping is pivotal in applications such as search and rescue and logistics, where crossing obstacles and enhancing mobility efficiency are critical. The Spring-Loaded Inverted Pendulum (SLIP) model leverages simplified spring-mass dynamics that naturally encode biologically plausible hopping motions, yet its performance degrades on irregular terrain due to idealized assumptions regarding contact and joint dynamics. Meanwhile, Reinforcement Learning (RL) can adapt to diverse and complex environments but often requires extensive data from unguided exploration. The complementary strengths of SLIP's physically grounded baseline and RL's adaptive capabilities motivate a hybrid framework that overcomes these individual limitations. We therefore propose Spring-loaded Reinforcement Learning (SRL), which integrates SLIP-based feedforward control signals with RL-driven real-time feedback, enabling continuous optimization of robotic jumping. Experimental results demonstrate that SRL can achieve more stable jumps with much less training time than the baseline method, maintaining an average position tracking error below 0.1 m and velocity tracking errors within +/-3% of the target values. Through bipedal and quadrupedal simulations of ground and stair jumping, as well as sim-to-sim and sim-to-real validations, SRL exhibits robust adaptability to various task requirements and environmental complexities, underscoring its potential for real-world deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper presents "Spring-loaded Reinforcement Learning" (SRL), a hybrid framework combining the SLIP model and reinforcement learning to enhance robotic jumping, achieving higher stability and faster training compared to prior methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the need to improve robotic jumping for applications like search and rescue and logistics, overcoming limitations of SLIP on complex terrains and RL's extensive data requirements.
AI.method: SRL combines SLIP-based feedforward dynamics with RL-driven feedback for continuous optimization, leveraging the strengths of both approaches to improve jumping performance.
AI.result: Experimental results show that SRL achieves stable jumps, reduced training time, position tracking error below 0.1 m, and velocity tracking errors within +/-3% of targets. It performs well in simulations for bipedal and quadrupedal robots, both on flat and irregular terrains.
AI.conclusion: The SRL framework effectively combines biologically inspired dynamics and adaptive learning, demonstrating robust adaptability and potential for real-world applications.

title: Forged Calamity: Benchmark for Cross-Domain Synthetic Disaster Detection in the Age of Diffusion
summary: The rapid advancement of text-to-image diffusion models has enabled the creation of highly photorealistic synthetic images that closely resemble real photographs, making it increasingly difficult to distinguish authentic content from AI-generated fabrications. This poses challenges for cybersecurity, digital forensics, and disaster response, where fake imagery of floods, fires, or earthquakes can spread misinformation or disrupt emergency operations. To address this, we introduce Forged Calamity, a benchmark dataset for synthetic disaster detection containing 30,000 images, including 6,000 real and 24,000 synthetic samples generated by four diffusion models. Comprehensive experiments across fine-tuned and zero-shot settings reveal consistent weaknesses in current forensic approaches. Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution but lose up to 50\% accuracy on unseen generators or disaster types, showing overfitting to model-specific artifacts. Zero-shot generalized detectors also struggle to maintain stable accuracy, with only limited resilience in a few representation-robust models. These findings highlight persistent generalization gaps and the urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods to ensure visual authenticity in the diffusion era.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a benchmark dataset, Forged Calamity, containing real and synthetic disaster images to evaluate and address weaknesses in detecting AI-generated fake disaster imagery.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to tackle the growing challenge of detecting photorealistic synthetic disaster images that can spread misinformation, disrupt essential operations, and pose cybersecurity risks.
AI.method: The authors created a dataset of 30,000 images (6,000 real and 24,000 synthetic from four diffusion models) and conducted experiments using fine-tuned and zero-shot detection approaches to analyze performance and generalization weaknesses.
AI.result: Fine-tuned detectors perform well in-distribution, but accuracy drops substantially in unseen scenarios. Zero-shot detectors also struggle, showing limited resilience only in specific robust models.
AI.conclusion: Current forensic approaches exhibit persistent generalization gaps, showing an urgent need for domain- and model-agnostic detection methods to ensure image authenticity amidst advancements in AI-generated content.

title: On Local Population-Risk Certificates
summary: This paper develops local certificates for population-risk increments around a current model. For a local candidate set \(\mathcal D\), the certificate is a two-sided confidence band for \(P({\ell_{θ+v}-\ell_θ})\) over \(v\in\mathcal D\). As an application, the upper endpoint of this band yields a risk-controlled update rule: an update is accepted only when its certified upper endpoint is nonpositive; otherwise the current model is retained.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes local certifications for population-risk increments and uses them for risk-controlled model updates.
AI.motivation: To ensure reliable updates to machine learning models by certifying risk increments with confidence bands.
AI.method: Develops two-sided confidence bands to estimate population-risk changes around a current model and applies them to accept or reject updates.
AI.result: Provides a risk-controlled update rule where updates with certified nonpositive upper endpoints can be accepted.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables precise control over model updates based on certified confidence bands for risk increments.

title: A Survey on Data-Driven Models for Soil Moisture Regression and Classification
summary: Soil Moisture (SM) modelling constitutes a complex spatiotemporal learning problem characterised by nonlinear environmental interactions, heterogeneous data sources, and limited ground observations. Physics-based approaches, such as water balance models, rely on explicit hydrological equations and high-quality inputs, but their computational cost and scalability limitations restrict large-scale deployment. Data-driven artificial intelligence (AI) methods have emerged as flexible alternatives, enabling the extraction of empirical relationships between soil moisture and environmental variables with reduced modelling assumptions. This work presents a structured survey of AI-based models for soil moisture estimation and classification. Existing approaches are organized into five categories: (a) statistical time-series models, (b) geostatistical methods (c) classical machine learning (ML) models, (d) Deep Learning (DL) models and (e) Probabilistic/Bayesian methods. These models leverage historical soil moisture records, meteorological variables, vegetation indices, topography, soil characteristics, and geolocation data to perform regression or classification tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys AI-based models for soil moisture estimation, categorizing them into five types: statistical time-series, geostatistical, classical ML, deep learning, and probabilistic/Bayesian methods.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of physics-based soil moisture modelling, such as high computational cost and scalability issues, by exploring the potential of AI models for accurate and scalable solutions.
AI.method: The study organizes and reviews AI-based approaches for soil moisture estimation and classification into five distinct categories, highlighting the types of data and tasks they handle.
AI.result: The review provides a comprehensive categorization of AI models using diverse variables like historical records, environmental factors, and geolocation data to model soil moisture.
AI.conclusion: AI offers flexible, data-driven alternatives to physics-based approaches for soil moisture modelling, enabling broader applicability and reduced assumptions in the estimation process.

title: A Composable CRDT Layer for Byzantine-Resilient Deterministic Reconstruction
summary:
Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) ensure Strong Eventual Consistency without coordination, but typically assume benign participants and rely on validation or exclusion to handle Byzantine behavior.
  We address this problem through deterministic state reconstruction: rather than deciding which updates are admissible, all accepted updates are incorporated, while only a subset contributes to the reconstructed state. We instantiate this approach in Melda, a non-intrusive delta-state CRDT for JSON documents, and show that its reconstruction model guarantees convergence even under arbitrary update injection: adversarial updates are either structurally rejected or treated as inputs to the reconstruction process.
  We formalize this model and prove that replicas deriving state from the same set of updates cannot diverge despite equivocation, omission, or message reordering. We further show that authentication, authorization, and confidentiality can be layered without affecting convergence.
  Overall, this approach suggests that Byzantine tolerance can be achieved by decoupling update propagation from state derivation, allowing agreement on updates to be handled independently by external dissemination or consensus mechanisms.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Melda, a Byzantine-tolerant CRDT for JSON documents, using deterministic state reconstruction to ensure convergence under adversarial conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of Byzantine tolerance in CRDTs while maintaining strong eventual consistency without requiring centralized coordination.
AI.method: Melda, a delta-state CRDT for JSON, uses a deterministic state reconstruction approach where all updates are incorporated, but only a subset contributes to the final state, ensuring convergence despite adversarial updates.
AI.result: It was proven that replicas using the same set of updates cannot diverge, even with message reordering, omission, or equivocation. Security layers like authentication can coexist without disrupting convergence.
AI.conclusion: By decoupling update propagation from state derivation, Byzantine tolerance can be achieved, allowing update agreements to rely on external mechanisms without affecting the CRDT's convergence properties.

title: Generative-Model Predictive Planning for Navigation in Partially Observable Environments
summary: Navigation in partially observable environments presents a significant challenge for autonomous agents, requiring effective decision-making with limited sensory information in unknown environments. Belief-based methods, particularly those using neural networks to approximate the belief space, often fail to capture the inherent multimodality of belief spaces, especially in high-dimensional cases with perceptual aliasing. While generative models present a compelling alternative, they typically require substantial data or expert demonstrations and lack explicit mechanisms for long-term planning. In this paper, we introduce BeliefDiffusion, a novel framework that combines the benefits of both generation and planning. BeliefDiffusion leverages diffusion models to explicitly characterize multimodal belief distributions and utilizes Model Predictive Control (MPC) to simultaneously plan ahead. It consists of two steps: (1) Imagining plausible environment configurations based on observation history and (2) Planning efficient navigation strategies across an aggregated configurations. Through extensive experiments in synthetic map environments, we demonstrate that BeliefDiffusion significantly outperforms both model-free reinforcement learning baselines and other generative approaches in navigation success rate and path efficiency. Our results validate that explicitly incorporating multimodal belief representations into planning enables more robust navigation in partially observable settings.

AI.tldr: BeliefDiffusion improves navigation in partially observable environments by combining diffusion models for multimodal belief representation and Model Predictive Control for planning.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in autonomous navigation where environments are partially observable, causing difficulties in decision-making due to perceptual aliasing.
AI.method: Proposes BeliefDiffusion, which uses diffusion models to capture multimodal belief distributions and applies Model Predictive Control for navigation planning.
AI.result: Experiments reveal BeliefDiffusion outperforms traditional reinforcement learning and generative methods across navigation success rate and path efficiency in synthetic environments.
AI.conclusion: Explicit incorporation of multimodal beliefs into planning enhances robustness and efficiency in navigating environments with limited observability.

title: No Two Developers Think Alike: How Problem-Solving Styles and Experience Shape Needs in Conversational Interaction with Copilot
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Software Maintenance and Evolution (ICSME), 2026
summary: Conversational LLM-based ``programming assistants'' provide a range of benefits to developers. However, recent studies demonstrate the variety in individual developers' needs regarding programming assistants, and challenges encountered by only specific groups of developers. In this study, we explore the role of cognitive diversity in shaping interactions with GitHub Copilot chat. Through a mixed-methods think aloud study with 27 professional developers and students, we characterize 5 distinct ``interaction modes'' and 10 underlying needs in developers' interactions, forming a conceptual model. We characterize links between these modes, needs, and developers' problem-solving styles and experience profiles, showing how cognitive diversity may shape developers' interactions. We provide insights and recommendations for researchers and practitioners on how to design, research, and employ programming assistants to better account for diverse developer needs.

AI.tldr: The study explores how cognitive diversity affects developer interactions with GitHub Copilot chat, identifying modes, needs, and providing recommendations for enhancing programming assistants.
AI.motivation: Investigate the effects of cognitive diversity on how developers use programming assistants like GitHub Copilot, addressing varied developer needs and challenges.
AI.method: A mixed-methods think-aloud study with 27 participants, including both professionals and students, to identify interaction modes, needs, and related Developer profiles.
AI.result: The study identified five distinct interaction modes, ten developer needs, and connections to diverse problem-solving styles and experience profiles.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive diversity significantly impacts developer interactions with programming assistants, requiring tailored designs to cater to diverse problem-solving styles and experiences.

title: MCompassRAG: Topic Metadata as a Semantic Compass for Paragraph-Level Retrieval
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems depend critically on how documents are chunked and searched. Fine-grained chunks can improve retrieval precision but expand the search space, increasing latency and cost; larger chunks reduce the number of candidates but make dense similarity less reliable, as the representation for each chunk mixes multiple topics and introduces more semantic noise. This trade-off becomes especially limiting in deep research tasks, where retrieval must be both fast and precise across large, heterogeneous corpora. We introduce MCompassRAG, a metadata-guided retrieval framework that uses topic-level signals as a semantic compass for selecting relevant evidence. Instead of relying only on cosine similarity between queries and noisy chunk embeddings, MCompassRAG enriches chunk representations with topic metadata in the same embedding space and trains a lightweight retriever through LLM-teacher distillation. At inference time, MCompassRAG performs topic-aware retrieval without additional LLM calls, improving both efficiency and evidence quality. Across six complex retrieval benchmarks, MCompassRAG improves information efficiency (IE) by 8.24% on average with over 5 times lower latency than the strongest efficient RAG baselines. Code is available on https://github.com/AmirAbaskohi/MCompassRAG.

AI.tldr: MCompassRAG enhances retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems by integrating metadata-guided, topic-aware retrieval for better efficiency and precision in deep research tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the trade-off in RAG systems between fine-grained document chunks improving precision but increasing latency and cost, and larger chunks reducing search precision by introducing semantic noise. This is especially problematic in deep research tasks requiring both precision and speed.
AI.method: MCompassRAG incorporates topic metadata into chunk embeddings, uses a lightweight retriever trained with LLM-teacher distillation, and performs topic-aware retrieval in a computationally efficient way without relying heavily on LLM calls at inference.
AI.result: The proposed system improves information efficiency by an average of 8.24% across six benchmarks, achieving over five times lower latency compared to leading RAG baselines.
AI.conclusion: MCompassRAG effectively balances precision and efficiency in RAG systems, offering a scalable solution for complex retrieval tasks while enhancing evidence quality and reducing computational demands.

title: Self-Supervised Mask-Aware Transformers for Fault-Tolerant FBG Force Sensing in Minimally Invasive Surgical Robotics
summary: In minimally invasive surgical robotics, catheter-scale Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors are promising due to their ability to estimate multi-dimensional forces by multiplexing several optical channels. However, deploying these compact multi-channel sensors introduces two critical engineering challenges: inherent nonlinear cross-axis coupling during complex deformations, and intermittent channel dropouts caused by fiber fractures in constrained workspaces. These compounding issues severely degrade force estimation. Existing fault-tolerant approaches rely on combinatorial model banks, which scale exponentially with the channel count and demand prohibitively expensive per-pattern calibration. In this paper, we propose a unified, self-supervised mask-aware Transformer that explicitly models channel availability to enable graceful degradation under diverse and dynamic sensor failures. The encoder is pretrained via masked-channel reconstruction on unlabeled data streams and fine-tuned for force regression using a balanced clean-and-corrupted-view objective alongside a dynamic corruption curriculum. Furthermore, a parallel uncertainty head, trained via heteroscedastic Gaussian negative log-likelihood, predicts per-axis confidence in a single forward pass, circumventing the overhead of multi-pass ensembles. Evaluated on a catheter-scale 8-channel FBG dataset, our single unified model achieves a nominal Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) of 0.0066~N and degrades gracefully to 0.0126~N under severe 4-channel failures. This significantly outperforms a comprehensive model bank of 255 per-pattern neural networks (0.0154~N at 4-channel loss) while eliminating pattern-specific calibration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a robust, self-supervised Transformer model for force estimation in catheter-scale Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) sensors, addressing challenges like cross-axis coupling and sensor failures.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to solve the engineering problems of nonlinear cross-axis coupling and intermittent channel dropouts in FBG sensors used in minimally invasive surgical robotics, which degrade force estimation.
AI.method: They introduce a mask-aware Transformer pretrained via masked-channel reconstruction and fine-tuned with a clean-and-corrupted-view objective and a dynamic corruption curriculum. An uncertainty head predicts per-axis confidence in a single forward pass.
AI.result: The model achieves a nominal RMSE of 0.0066 N and gracefully degrades to 0.0126 N under 4-channel failures, outperforming traditional model banks across various failure scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Transformer model offers a scalable, efficient, and fault-tolerant solution for force estimation in multi-channel FBG sensors, eliminating the need for extensive model banks and per-pattern calibration.

title: Rethinking Text-to-Image as Semantic-Aware Data Augmentation for Indoor Scene Recognition
summary: In the realm of computer vision, indoor image recognition presents challenges due to the intricate interplay of lighting conditions, occlusions, and diverse object arrangements within confined spaces. To address the lacks of training indoor images, we introduce a novel approach leveraging Stable Diffusion (SD) for the generation of synthetic images, which serve as a powerful data augmentation tool. The utilization of SD offers a principled framework for synthesizing diverse and realistic indoor scenes, thereby enriching the training data pool for robust indoor image recognition models. Experimental findings on the MIT Indoor Scene dataset reveal the potential of our proposed approach in enhancing the training of deep models when authentic data is limited. Furthermore, to prevent the misuse of SD synthetic images, we introduce a counter measure based on DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE). The powerful DIRE presentation enables training robust classifiers only using lightweight deep models. Experiments show that our approach can perfectly recognize SD generated images with the accuracy of 100% using MobilenetV3.

AI.tldr: This paper uses Stable Diffusion (SD) to generate synthetic indoor images for data augmentation in training image recognition models, and introduces a lightweight classifier leveraging Diffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE) for detecting SD-generated images.
AI.motivation: Indoor image recognition faces challenges due to lighting, occlusion, and diverse object arrangements, coupled with the lack of sufficient training data.
AI.method: The paper employs Stable Diffusion (SD) to generate synthetic indoor image data and applies a DIffusion Reconstruction Error (DIRE)-based approach for robust classifier training.
AI.result: Experiments on the MIT Indoor Scene dataset show improved recognition performance and 100% accuracy in detecting SD-generated images using MobilenetV3.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic SD images effectively enhance training datasets for indoor image recognition while DIRE-based classifiers ensure ethical use by accurately detecting SD-generated images.

title: Generalised Eigenvalue Geometry of Semantic Adversarial Attacks
summary: Recent empirical work shows that semantically equivalent paraphrases can fool financial sentiment classifiers: although a paraphrase remains close to the original under a strong reference embedding, it may shift the target model's representation enough to change the predicted class. Existing robustness theory either assumes a single-model threat model or focuses mainly on empirical attack algorithms. We develop a continuous local model of semantic paraphrase perturbations that captures this two-model structure. We show that the worst-case local displacement of the target representation, subject to a proxy-model budget, is governed by the largest generalised eigenvalue of a matrix pencil $(A,B)$ constructed from the Jacobians of the two embedding maps. The resulting attackability index $λ^*(x)$ is intrinsic to the local paraphrase geometry and the chosen embedders, yields a closed-form prediction-flip condition for affine readouts, and supports conservative population and finite-sample attackability certificates. For uniform control over classes of affine readouts, we derive a distribution-free VC bound for binary attackability indicators and a scale-sensitive margin bound based on an attackability-adjusted margin that subtracts a local geometric penalty from the standard classifier margin. We also connect the continuous theory to discrete paraphrase search, identify an asymmetry between successful and unsuccessful finite searches, and give a covering condition under which the discrete and continuous settings agree. Finally, we propose an empirical verification framework using soft-token relaxations and generated paraphrase sets to assess the local eigenvalue geometry, prediction-flip condition, and finite-search approximation on a deployed financial-text classifier.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a theoretical and practical framework for understanding and addressing the vulnerability of financial sentiment classifiers to semantic paraphrases.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in robustness literature for financial sentiment classifiers that are susceptible to semantic paraphrase attacks, which can lead to misclassifications.
AI.method: The authors propose a continuous local model of semantic paraphrase perturbations. They use a matrix-pencil representation involving the Jacobians of two embedding maps to calculate an attackability index that predicts paraphrase-induced misclassifications.
AI.result: The study introduces an attackability index, provides analytical tools like eigenvalue-based prediction-flip conditions, and derives bounds and certificates for classifier robustness. They also connect the theory to practical discrete paraphrase attacks and propose an empirical evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: The paper offers both theoretical guarantees and practical tools to assess and improve the robustness of financial sentiment classifiers, especially for semantic paraphrase attacks.

title: Enhanced Graph Neural Networks using K-Hop Gaussian Diffusion
summary: Most graph neural network (GNN) cores rely on graph convolutions, typically implemented as message passing between direct (single-hop) neighbors. In many real-world graphs, edges can be noisy or poorly defined, limiting information propagation to local neighborhoods. Existing diffusion kernels, such as Personalized PageRank (PPR) and Heat Kernel, alleviate this issue through global propagation, but still struggle with complex local structures and distant node noise. To address these limitations, we propose a K-Hop Gaussian (KHG) diffusion kernel as a preprocessing module for graph data. KHG introduces multi-hop diffusion with Gaussian weighting for remote nodes, balancing local and global information propagation before applying standard GNNs. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that KHG significantly outperforms traditional message-passing GNNs, as well as PPR and Heat Kernel diffusion, particularly in noisy or structurally complex graphs.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces K-Hop Gaussian (KHG), a preprocessing diffusion kernel that addresses noise and complex structures in graph data, outperforming standard graph convolution methods.
AI.motivation: Graphs with noisy or poorly defined edges and complex structures limit the effectiveness of conventional graph convolution methods implemented in GNNs.
AI.method: Development of the K-Hop Gaussian (KHG) diffusion kernel, which uses multi-hop diffusion with Gaussian weighting to balance local and global information propagation.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show that KHG surpasses message-passing GNNs, PPR, and Heat Kernel diffusion, particularly under challenging conditions like noisy graphs.
AI.conclusion: KHG provides enhanced information propagation in graphs, making it a promising preprocessing approach for improving performance in GNNs.

title: On the Notions of Bounded Bypass, and How to Make any Deadlock-Free MUTEX Protocol Satisfy One of Them
summary:
In the literature on mutual exclusion, bounded bypass has been used for a long time as a strengthening of starvation-freedom, but, to the best of our knowledge, it still lacks a satisfying definition as a liveness property on its own. Moreover, we have encountered MUTEX protocols for which this notion needs to be slightly weakened in order to be met.
  To solve these issues, we first provide a formal definition of bounded bypass (that also corrects a previous definition from Raynal) and then introduce the notions of post-doorway and intermittent bounded bypass, two liveness properties that lie between starvation-freedom and bounded bypass. Essentially, intermittent bounded bypass weakens bounded bypass by ignoring the possible bypasses that may happen during the execution of a certain finite set of write operations to shared registers. Orthogonally, post-doorway bounded bypass ignores the bypasses that may happen during a finite initial phase of the lock protocol.
  Furthermore, we study an algorithm proposed by Yoah Bar-David in 1998 to enhance the liveness properties of any deadlock-free MUTEX protocol and prove that: (1) in the setting of atomic registers, this algorithm upgrades any deadlock-free mutual exclusion protocol to a bounded bypass one, with a bound that is quadratic in the number of processes; and (2) in the setting of safe and regular registers, the very same algorithm ensures the intermittent version of bounded bypass, still with a quadratic (but slightly different) bound.
  Finally, we provide logical formulae for the different notions of bounded bypass defined in this paper and use them to confirm all claims made here, by using model checking. This had a positive impact on the theoretical development of the work, since it allowed us to identify and correct small mistakes/ambiguities in definitions and proofs.

AI.tldr: This paper formalizes and extends the notion of bounded bypass in mutual exclusion protocols, introduces two new properties—post-doorway and intermittent bounded bypass—and examines an algorithm for enhancing liveness properties.
AI.motivation: The motivation was to address the vague and incomplete definitions of bounded bypass as a liveness property in mutual exclusion protocols, and to resolve practical challenges in ensuring the property in existing protocols.
AI.method: The authors provided formal definitions for bounded bypass and its variants, introduced two intermediate liveness properties, analyzed an algorithm by Yoah Bar-David, and validated their theoretical claims using logical formulae and model checking.
AI.result: The study demonstrated that Bar-David's algorithm upgrades any deadlock-free mutual exclusion protocol to bounded bypass in atomic registers and intermittent bounded bypass in safe and regular registers, both with quadratic bounds.
AI.conclusion: By offering formal definitions, intermediate properties, and algorithmic enhancements, this work contributes to the understanding and practical implementation of bounded bypass in mutual exclusion protocols.

title: Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation for GUI Agents
summary: Improving GUI agents typically relies on behavior cloning on expert trajectories. However, as the current policy deviates from the expert policy, it inevitably encounters policy-induced off-trajectory states during closed-loop execution, i.e., states that fall outside the expert trajectories. Since expert trajectories provide no demonstrations for these unseen states, such states receive no effective supervision, leaving the policy unable to select the correct action. To close this supervision gap, we propose Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation (SGCD), an iterative self-improvement framework. SGCD first runs the plain policy without skill guidance for a few steps to reach realistic off-trajectory states. From these states, a skill-guided policy then completes the task and produces successful continuations, which are mixed with expert trajectories to supply supervision over policy-induced off-trajectory states. The skills are extracted from both successful and failed rollouts, consisting of Continuation Plans, Critical Targets, Failure Traps, and Success Criteria. On OSWorld-Verified, SGCD improves the success rate of three base models from the low-30\% range to over 50\%, demonstrating its effectiveness and generality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Skill-Guided Continuation Distillation (SGCD) to address the challenge of off-trajectory states in GUI agents, significantly improving their performance.
AI.motivation: To overcome the issue of policy deviation in GUI agents that leads to off-trajectory states where no effective supervision exists due to the lack of expert trajectories.
AI.method: The proposed SGCD framework mixes successful continuations, generated by a skill-guided policy, with expert trajectories to enhance supervision for off-trajectory states. Skills are derived from successful and failed rollouts.
AI.result: The SGCD framework increased the success rate of three base models on OSWorld-Verified from low-30% to over 50%, showcasing its general applicability and improvement.
AI.conclusion: SGCD is a powerful self-improvement framework that effectively bridges the supervision gap for off-trajectory states, enabling GUI agents to perform better in real-world tasks.

title: Runtime Compliance Verification for AI Agents
summary: AI agents now handle personal data through tool use, function calls, and multi turn dialogue, which can create obligations under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Current testing practices mainly rely on offline red teaming or static prompt review, but they do not guarantee at runtime that agent behavior follows regulatory rules. We propose C-Trace (Compliance Trace based Runtime Agent Conformance Enforcement), a verification framework that: (i) expresses a subset of GDPR requirements, including consent, purpose limitation, data minimization, and the right to erasure, as formal policy predicates over agent execution traces; (ii) uses a runtime monitor that intercepts every tool invocation and model output and rejects non-compliant actions; and (iii) tests the agent with attack dialogues, including DSPy generated prompts and verbatim prompts from red teaming corpora, that try to induce violations. We evaluate the framework on four case studies reframed to GDPR. Under 10 percent per-category extractor noise, including drop-out and over-typing, the monitor keeps the attack success rate at less than or equal to 12 percent, below the baselines we compare against, and false positives at less than or equal to 16 percent, and reaches 0 percent ASR under perfect extraction.

AI.tldr: The paper presents C-Trace, a framework for ensuring AI agents' compliance with GDPR rules at runtime.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in current practices that fail to ensure AI agents adhere to GDPR requirements during actual operation.
AI.method: C-Trace defines GDPR rules as formal policy predicates over agent traces, employs a runtime monitor to reject non-compliant actions, and utilizes attack dialogues to evaluate agent behavior.
AI.result: The proposed system achieves a low attack success rate of ≤12% (or 0% under perfect extraction) and low false positives (≤16%) even under noisy conditions.
AI.conclusion: C-Trace effectively enforces GDPR compliance during runtime, outperforming existing baselines in accuracy and robustness.

title: Speech-Driven End-to-End Language Discrimination towards Chinese Dialects
summary: Language discrimination among similar languages, varieties, and dialects is a challenging natural language processing task. The traditional text-driven focus leads to poor results. In this paper, we explore the effectiveness of speech-driven features towards language discrimination among Chinese dialects. First, we systematically explore the appropriateness of speech-driven MFCC features towards CNN-based language discrimination. Then, we design an end-to-end speech recognition model based on HMM-DNN to predict Chinese dialect words. We adopt attention to extract the discriminative words related to different Chinese dialects. Finally, through a CNN, we combine the word-level embedding and the MFCC-based features. Evaluation of two benchmark Chinese dialect corpora shows the appropriateness and effectiveness of the proposed speech-driven approach to fine-grained Chinese dialect discrimination compared to the state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the use of speech-driven features for distinguishing between Chinese dialects, integrating MFCC features and word-level embeddings with CNNs.
AI.motivation: Poor results from traditional text-driven approaches in identifying Chinese dialects motivate exploring speech-driven features.
AI.method: Speech-driven MFCC features are tested with CNNs, alongside an end-to-end HMM-DNN model that predicts dialect-specific words, with attention mechanism to highlight distinguishing words. A combination of MFCC and word-level embeddings is then implemented.
AI.result: Experimental results on two Chinese dialect corpora show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods for fine-grained dialect discrimination.
AI.conclusion: The proposed speech-driven approach effectively handles fine-grained Chinese dialect discrimination, demonstrating its potential over traditional text-based methods.

title: DNN Koopman-Based Deviation Compensation for UGV Path Tracking Control on Coupled Slope and Potholed Road
summary: Unmanned ground vehicles (UGVs) operating in off-road scenarios are confronted with complex terrain disturbances that can substantially degrade path tracking performance. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a deep neural network (DNN) Koopman-based deviation compensation strategy for UGV path tracking control. Firstly, based on the vehicle dynamic function on coupled slope, an adaptive forgetting recursive least squares method with decoupled error terms is designed to estimate tire cornering stiffness. On this basis, a Laguerre model predictive control (LMPC) path tracking control strategy is designed by incorporating Laguerre functions, which can reduce computational resource usage while maintaining reliable tracking performance across different coupled slope scenarios. Then, by integrating Koopman operator theory with DNN, a DNN Koopman (DK) path deviation compensation method is proposed, which significantly improves the path tracking accuracy of UGV under potholed road disturbances. Furthermore, an event-triggered parallel cooperative (EPC) compensation mechanism that couples LMPC with DK is established based on compensation activation criteria and credibility verification. This mechanism improves path tracking accuracy on potholed road while ensuring the feasibility of overall steering command and stability of vehicle after DK compensation. Finally, a hardware-in-the-loop (HiL) experimental platform is constructed for validation. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed UGV path tracking strategy improves tracking performance by more than 11.5% across multiple operating conditions.

AI.tldr: A new deep neural network Koopman-based compensation strategy improves off-road unmanned ground vehicle (UGV) path tracking accuracy by 11.5%.
AI.motivation: UGVs face significant path tracking degradation due to complex terrain disturbances in off-road environments, necessitating advanced control methods.
AI.method: Developed a Koopman-based path tracking strategy combining adaptive estimation of tire stiffness, Laguerre model predictive control (LMPC), and a DNN for path deviation compensation.
AI.result: The proposed approach enhanced path tracking performance by over 11.5% in hardware-in-the-loop experiments, especially under challenging road conditions.
AI.conclusion: This integrated strategy effectively improves UGV path tracking performance and system stability on uneven terrains, achieving reliable operation in off-road conditions.

title: MolmoMotion: Forecasting Point Trajectories in 3D with Language Instruction
summary: Motion forecasting is central to visual intelligence: agents must anticipate how objects will move in order to plan actions, reason about physical interactions, and synthesize realistic futures. We argue that 3D points in world coordinates provide a general representation that is class-agnostic, view-stable, compact, and directly useful for downstream tasks. We formalize the task of goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting: given a short visual history, a set of 3D query points on an object of interest, and a language description of the intended goal, the model predicts the future 3D trajectory of each point. We introduce a full stack to study this task at scale: (1) MolmoMotion-1M is a large corpus of action-described, object-grounded 3D point trajectories annotated from 1.16M unconstrained videos; (2) PointMotionBench is a human-verified benchmark spanning 111 object categories and 61 motion types; and (3) MolmoMotion is a general motion forecasting model that supports both autoregressive coordinate prediction and flow-matching-based trajectory generation. MolmoMotion accurately predicts diverse motion patterns with different language instructions, and significantly outperforms existing motion prediction baselines on PointMotionBench. Finally, we show that the learned 3D motion prior transfers well to downstream applications: it improves training efficiency and generalization for robot manipulation, and its predicted trajectories provide effective motion guidance for generative models to synthesize videos with more realistic object motion.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a goal-conditioned 3D point motion forecasting approach using a 3D-centric representation to predict object motions, aided by datasets, benchmarks, and a new model.
AI.motivation: To enable visual systems to accurately anticipate object motions for better action planning, interaction reasoning, and realistic future synthesis, the need for a universal, class-agnostic representation arose.
AI.method: The authors formalized 3D point motion forecasting and developed a new dataset (MolmoMotion-1M), a benchmark suite (PointMotionBench), and a model (MolmoMotion) combining autoregressive and flow-based prediction techniques.
AI.result: MolmoMotion outperformed current baselines on PointMotionBench and excelled in applications such as robot manipulation and video synthesis by providing realistic and goal-aligned future predictions.
AI.conclusion: A 3D points-based representation for motion forecasting proves effective for both predictive accuracy and downstream applications, highlighting its potential in advancing physical understanding and planning.

title: The Implicit Bias of Steepest Descent with Mini-batch Stochastic Gradient
summary: A variety of widely used optimization methods like SignSGD and Muon can be interpreted as instances of steepest descent under different norm-induced geometries. In this work, we study the implicit bias of mini-batch stochastic steepest descent in multi-class classification, characterizing how batch size, momentum, and variance reduction shape the limiting max-margin behavior and convergence rates under general entry-wise and Schatten-$p$ norms. We show that, without momentum, worst-case convergence and successful classification can only be guaranteed with full-batch gradient. In contrast, momentum enables small-batch convergence to an approximate max-margin solution through a batch-momentum trade-off, though it slows convergence. This approach provides fully explicit, dimension-free rates that improve upon prior results. Moreover, we prove that variance reduction can recover the exact full-batch implicit bias for any batch size, albeit at a slower convergence rate. Finally, we further investigate the batch-size-one steepest descent without momentum, and reveal its convergence to a fundamentally different bias via a concrete data example, which reveals a key limitation of purely stochastic updates. Overall, our unified analysis clarifies when stochastic optimization aligns with full-batch behavior, and paves the way for perform deeper explorations of the training behavior of stochastic gradient steepest descent algorithms.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes the implicit bias and behavior of stochastic steepest descent algorithms in multi-class classification, focusing on how factors like batch size, momentum, and variance reduction influence performance and convergence.
AI.motivation: To understand the theoretical underpinnings and practical implications of stochastic steepest descent methods under varying geometries, particularly addressing how different factors control convergence and implicit bias.
AI.method: The study involves characterizing convergence rates and max-margin behavior under diverse norm-induced geometries, analyzing momentum's effects, and exploring variance reduction strategies.
AI.result: The paper finds that full-batch gradients are necessary for worst-case guarantees without momentum, while small-batch convergence is viable with momentum but slower. Additionally, variance reduction bridges small- and full-batch methods at a cost to speed, and batch-size-one methods reveal distinct limitations.
AI.conclusion: The unified analysis helps in understanding how stochastic optimization can emulate full-batch behavior and provides new avenues for improving and interpreting stochastic gradient methods.

title: ASTRA: A Scalable Next-Generation ATCO Training Simulator with Autonomous Simpilots
summary: Air Traffic Control Operators (ATCOs) are vital in ensuring the safe, orderly, and efficient flow of air traffic, yet training capacity is constrained by reliance on specialized human trainers known as simpilots, who must role-play both pilots and ATCOs in a simulated airspace. Existing automated solutions rely on Western-centric speech models that perform poorly in Singaporean operational contexts, with off-the-shelf systems exhibiting Word Error Rates (WER) of up to 107.80% on Singaporean-accented aviation speech. We introduce ASTRA, an end-to-end training simulator that automates these simpilot roles through a pipeline that transcribes ATCO speech, interprets instructions, and generates appropriate pilot and ATCO responses using locally adapted voice models. Our fine-tuned Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) pipeline reduces WER to 23.45%, substantially outperforming existing approaches in this domain. Beyond traffic simulation, ASTRA incorporates an AI-assisted performance evaluation framework that assesses trainee radiotelephony communications across accuracy, brevity, and completeness, achieving post-optimization scores of 91.7%, 88.2%, and 86.9%, respectively. Built on open-source foundations such as DSPy and Unsloth, this approach enables scalable, standardized ATCO assessment while reducing instructor workload.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ASTRA, a training simulator aimed to address issues in Singaporean ATCO training, achieving significant improvements in automatic speech recognition and trainee performance assessment.
AI.motivation: The training of Air Traffic Control Operators (ATCOs) is restricted by dependence on human trainers or simpilots, and existing automated solutions fail in Singaporean contexts due to high Word Error Rates (WER) in aviation speech.
AI.method: ASTRA employs a locally adapted ASR pipeline to transcribe ATCO speech, interpret instructions, and simulate pilot and ATCO roles. It integrates AI-driven evaluation of trainee performance using open-source tools.
AI.result: ASTRA improves ASR performance, reducing WER from 107.80% to 23.45%, and achieves high evaluation metrics for accuracy, brevity, and completeness (91.7%, 88.2%, 86.9%, respectively).
AI.conclusion: ASTRA provides a scalable and efficient solution for ATCO training with localized speech recognition and automated performance evaluation, significantly enhancing training outcomes while reducing the reliance on human trainers.

title: Spotlight: Synergizing Seed Exploration and Spot GPUs for DiT RL Post-Training
summary:
Reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) is prohibitively expensive, requiring thousands of high-end GPUs. Existing works explore two directions to reduce cost: seed exploration improves training convergence by selecting high-contrast samples, yet adds compute to the critical path; spot GPUs offer 69--77\% lower cost, yet sit idle during training because DiT rollouts finish nearly simultaneously, which prevents LLM-style pipelining of rollout with training. Spot preemptions further break Sequence Parallelism (SP) groups, fragmenting GPU topology.
  We present Spotlight, the first system that harvests spot GPUs for DiT RL post-training. Spotlight rests on two key insights we devise: (1)~we show that exploration can tolerate stale model weights because exploration that uses the model weights from the previous iteration preserves the relative ranking of random seeds, allowing exploration to run on idle spot GPUs during training. (2)~SP reconfiguration can reuse on-node state, reducing group recovery from minutes to sub-second launches. Built on these insights, Spotlight introduces three techniques: a bandit-based exploration planner that maximizes reward variance within the training time budget, elastic sequence parallelism that reconfigures SP groups on the fly via persistent schedulers and intra-node weight copying, and a preemption-aware pull-based request scheduler that balances load and commits in-flight state upon preemption. We implement Spotlight on the open-source RL platform ROLL and evaluate it on Qwen-Image post-training. Spotlight reaches the same target validation score $4\times$ faster than baselines, reducing total cost by $1.4$-$6.4\times$ while achieving superior image quality on DeepSeek-OCR and Geneval datasets with resolution $512\times512$ and $1280\times1280$.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Spotlight, a system for accelerating reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) by utilizing spot GPUs effectively, reducing costs and improving speed.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning post-training of Diffusion Transformers requires substantial computation resources, making it prohibitively expensive. Existing cost-reduction strategies like seed exploration and spot GPUs have limitations such as increased overhead or idle GPUs. The paper aims to address these inefficiencies.
AI.method: The authors propose Spotlight, relying on two primary insights: (1) exploration can use stale model weights without losing effectiveness, allowing it to run independently on spot GPUs, and (2) SP reconfiguration with on-node state reuse enables faster recovery in case of preemptions. Spotlight incorporates a bandit-based exploration planner, elastic sequence parallelism, and a preemption-aware scheduler.
AI.result: Spotlight achieves the same target validation score four times faster than the baseline methods. It reduces total costs by 1.4x to 6.4x and delivers superior image quality on datasets like DeepSeek-OCR and Geneval, with resolutions up to 1280x1280.
