We discover this through LLMZero, a system where LLM agents search over training trajectories via tree search, diagnosing pathologies at each checkpoint and proposing coordinated multi-parameter transitions. Across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, LLMZero discovers strategies that improve over the base model by 9% to 140% relative and over grid search by 6% to 15% relative, consistently outperforming random search and the skill-based agent. The structural principle transfers across tasks, providing an explanation for why discovered strategies take qualitatively different forms yet share similar parameter dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LLMZero, a system leveraging tree search to optimize training trajectories for reinforcement learning (RL) post-training, demonstrating substantial performance improvements across tasks.
AI.motivation: To uncover the dynamics of capacity and regularization parameter behaviors during RL post-training and improve upon fixed-schedule strategies through adaptable, multi-stage training.
AI.method: This study uses LLMZero, a system where large language model (LLM) agents perform a tree search to explore training trajectories, diagnose issues at checkpoints, and propose coordinated adjustments for multiple parameters.
AI.result: LLMZero achieves 9%-140% relative improvements over the base model and 6%-15% over grid search across 4 diverse GRPO tasks, consistently outperforming random search and skill-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The discovered strategies validate the importance of non-stationary parameter dynamics in RL post-training and demonstrate transferable principles that explain the varied forms yet shared dynamics of effective parameter adjustments.

title: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
summary: To achieve deeper safety alignment for large language models (LLMs), recent efforts have studied how to push safety interventions earlier into the pretraining stage, primarily by filtering unsafe data or rewriting it into safer forms. We argue that pretraining-stage alignment should go beyond making the data safe: LLMs may compose seemingly benign knowledge and capabilities into unsafe behaviors. To this end, we propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, a pretraining-stage alignment method which regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring directly into language modeling, establishing a foundational capability that is subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training. Our experiments with 1.7B models pretrained on FineWeb-Edu show that Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces the success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Complementary to our real-world experiments, we also introduce a fully controlled synthetic environment, MedSafetyWorld, with a clear definition of safety and a reasoning structure under which models can easily generalize unsafe behaviors from safe data. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld further demonstrate a clear advantage of Safety Reflection Pretraining in preventing models from acting on unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data, compared with data filtering and rewriting. Taken together, our findings suggest that pretraining alignment should not only make the training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes "Safety Reflection Pretraining," a method to align large language models (LLMs) during pretraining by inserting safety reflections into the data, improving safety and reducing unsafe behaviors.
AI.motivation: Current efforts in aligning LLMs focus mainly on filtering or rewriting unsafe data but fail to address situations where models generate unsafe behavior from seemingly safe data. This gap necessitates a method that shapes the inherent safety-related behavior of the model during pretraining.
AI.method: Safety Reflection Pretraining incorporates short safety reflections into the pretraining corpus to integrate self-monitoring into language modeling. This foundational alignment capability is later reinforced through compatible post-training approaches.
AI.result: Experiments with 1.7B models on the FineWeb-Edu dataset show improved safety classification, reduced vulnerability to attacks, and effectiveness over data filtering and rewriting in both real-world and synthetic environments.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining alignment should extend beyond just ensuring safe training data; it should influence the types of behavior models acquire from the data, as demonstrated by the advantages of the proposed approach.

title: TW-LegalBench: Measuring Taiwanese Legal Understanding
comment: 10 pages, 2 figures, To appear in ICAIL 2026
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown impressive capabilities across diverse tasks, yet their performance on jurisdiction-specific legal reasoning remains underexplored. We present TW-LegalBench that utilizes Taiwanese legal system's rich official corpus open to the public to fill the gap in evaluating LLMs on Taiwanese law, among common-law benchmarks that focus on English sources and civil-law benchmarks focusing on sources of Simplified Chinese. TW-LegalBench comprises three task types: (1) over 16,000 multiple-choice questions (MCQs) across five years of official examinations in 18 professional domains; (2) 117 open-ended essay questions (OEQs) from examinations for legal professionals with official scoring rubrics; and (3) more than 14,000 legal judgment prediction (LJP) instances covering hundreds of crime categories. We evaluate 13 LLMs using accuracy for MCQs, a decomposed LLM-as-Judge framework based on the scoring rubric points for OEQs, and metrics for sentencing accuracy and statute citation for LJP. Our results reveal that top-performing models exceed the passing threshold for qualified lawyers (passing rate: 11%) but fall short of that for judges and prosecutors (passing rate: 1~2%). For LJP, while models demonstrate reasonable verdict type accuracy and sentence prediction capability, they struggle to cite exact legal articles. These findings highlight that reliable legal text generation remains challenging for LLMs, even though their performance on qualification examinations approaches human level.

AI.tldr: TW-LegalBench evaluates LLMs on Taiwanese legal reasoning through MCQs, OEQs, and LJP tasks, revealing promising yet imperfect results.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of assessment of LLMs' capability in Taiwanese legal reasoning within jurisdiction-specific benchmarks.
AI.method: Develop TW-LegalBench with MCQs, essay questions with scoring rubrics, and legal judgment prediction tasks; evaluate 13 LLMs using performance metrics.
AI.result: Top-performing models surpass lawyer qualification thresholds but fall short for judge/prosecutor levels; struggle with exact legal article citation.
AI.conclusion: LLMs approach human-level performance on legal exams but face challenges in precise legal text generation, emphasizing room for improvement.

title: Space Is Intelligence: Neural Semigroup Superposition for Riemannian Metric Generation
summary: Traditional approaches place intelligence in the agent, whether as a learned policy or a search procedure. We instead place intelligence in the space itself: a scene induces a Riemannian metric on the configuration manifold, and action reduces to following the geodesics of that metric rather than invoking a separate planner or collision checker. A single Encoder-Router network realizes this idea through three complementary parameter groups -- frame parameters that orient the generators, modulation parameters that govern their spatial propagation, and basic coefficients that determine their strength. These groups combine through a shared semigroup-superposition mechanism to produce a single Riemannian metric field, yielding a compact architecture whose geometry scales naturally with scene complexity. Trained on a single two-obstacle scene, the model demonstrates robust zero-shot generalization across unseen obstacle configurations, with orders-of-magnitude separation between collision-free and obstacle-penetrating path costs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an innovative approach where intelligence is embedded in the configuration space itself through a Riemannian metric, enabling geodesic-based action rather than traditional planning or collision checking.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional intelligence architectures, which depend on agents with learned policies or search procedures, by enabling scene-induced intelligence in the configuration manifold.
AI.method: The authors use an Encoder-Router network with three parameter groups (frame parameters, modulation parameters, and basic coefficients) to generate a unified Riemannian metric field for guiding action.
AI.result: The system, trained on a two-obstacle scene, achieves robust zero-shot generalization to new obstacle configurations with significant differentiation in path costs between collision-free and obstacle paths.
AI.conclusion: Embedding intelligence in the Riemannian space provides a scalable, compact, and efficient architecture for decision-making in dynamic and complex scenes.

title: On-Manifold Variational Learning with Heat-Kernel Priors
summary: Learning unsupervised representations of medical imaging cohorts can reveal clinically meaningful prototypes without expert labels, which are often noisy and fail to capture true pathological heterogeneity. However, existing deep latent-variable models estimate Gaussian mixture priors via Euclidean averaging, producing prototypes that drift off the curved data manifold and degenerate as the number of sub-populations grows. We propose a manifold-anchored variational framework built on a geometry-aware Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, whose M-step selects each sub-population prototype as the graph medoid with the highest diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, ensuring that every prototype remains on-manifold. A Dirichlet energy regularizer enforces geometric smoothness of the latent space, and a per-sub-population uncertainty score enables label-free quality assessment. \rev{The manifold-anchored EM is a general-purpose geometric tool that extends standard EM and applies readily to other latent-variable models beyond this setting.} On cardiac scar and brain MRI benchmarks, our framework attains the highest accuracy among all compared methods, produces the sharpest prototypes reported to date, and remains stable at large sub-population counts where all baselines degenerate.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a geometry-aware unsupervised learning framework to improve prototype generation in medical imaging, overcoming limitations of traditional deep models.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of noisy expert labels and pathological heterogeneity in medical imaging analysis, while mitigating prototype degeneration from Gaussian mixture priors.
AI.method: The paper proposes a manifold-anchored variational framework with a geometry-aware EM algorithm. Prototypes are selected using graph medoid diffusion centrality on a heat-kernel-weighted latent graph, with a Dirichlet energy regularizer for smoothness and an uncertainty score for quality assessment.
AI.result: The method achieves the highest accuracy on cardiac and brain MRI benchmarks compared to existing approaches, generates sharp prototypes, and maintains stability even with numerous sub-populations.
AI.conclusion: The manifold-anchored EM framework is a robust, general-purpose geometric approach suitable for various latent-variable models, offering significant improvements in medical imaging analysis.

title: TS-Fault: Benchmarking Time Series Forecasters Against Structural Faults
summary: Time series forecasting (TSF) underpins consequential decisions in energy, transportation, finance, and healthcare, yet TSF models are almost universally ranked by a single number (e.g., average error) on clean held-out data, under the implicit assumption that it predicts deployed reliability. However, real faults are not i.i.d noise but structured events with temporal shape, broken cross-variable dependencies, regime change coupled with missingness, and causal propagation across a sensing pipeline. Treating TSF robustness as a data-quality problem, we present TS-Fault, a benchmark that evaluates forecasting models under explicit, parameterized fault scenarios with controllable semantic difficulty. TS-Fault organizes recurring failures into four modes along two orthogonal axes (observation- vs mechanism-level; univariate vs multivariate) and injects each fault into the most prediction-critical window via a unified importance score. This design enables robustness to be tested against the structures models actually rely on, rather than reduced to generic noise sensitivity. We evaluate 21 models across 6 datasets, 4 modes, and 5 difficulty levels under a paired clean/corrupt protocol. The results reveal three findings that contradict common leaderboard intuition: (i) clean-data accuracy anti-correlates with robustness; (ii) clean rankings are preserved under observation-level faults but reshuffled under mechanism-level faults; and (iii) all catastrophic failures occur under mechanism-level faults, with foundation models achieving the highest clean-data accuracy yet exhibiting the greatest fragility. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Ray-zyy/TS-Fault.

AI.tldr: This paper presents TS-Fault, a benchmark for evaluating time series forecasting robustness by simulating real-world fault scenarios. It reveals contradictions between clean-data accuracy and robustness.
AI.motivation: Time series forecasting often assumes that performance on clean data predicts real-world robustness. This paper challenges that assumption by examining structured, real-world faults that affect forecasting reliability.
AI.method: The authors introduce TS-Fault, a benchmark that evaluates robustness of forecasting models by injecting controllable faults with specific semantic difficulties. They categorize faults into four modes based on axes (observation vs mechanism-level, univariate vs multivariate) and inject faults into critical windows.
AI.result: The study evaluated 21 models across 6 datasets and multiple fault scenarios, finding that clean-data accuracy does not correlate with robustness and that mechanism-level faults are especially challenging and yield catastrophic failures.
AI.conclusion: The paper challenges reliance on clean-data accuracy for assessing forecasting models, highlighting that mechanism-level faults expose severe robustness issues even in high-performing models.

title: MOLAR: Learning Multimodal Molecular Representations from Noisy Labels
summary: Motivation: Noisy labels are a common challenge in molecular property prediction because molecular annotations are often obtained from assays, curated databases, or weak annotation pipelines rather than directly observed clean biological states. Treating recorded labels as reliable supervision can cause models to memorize corrupted observations and learn misleading molecular evidence. In multimodal molecular representation learning, this issue can be amplified by graph-text fusion or alignment, which may propagate label-induced errors across modalities. Results: We propose MOLAR, a noise-aware framework for learning multimodal molecular representations from noisy labels. MOLAR separates latent clean-property inference from recorded-label observation: graph and text views contribute residual evidence to a clean-property distribution, and a categorical label-observation channel maps this distribution to recorded labels for training. This formulation derives posterior label reliability and modality-specific molecular evidence from the model. Experiments on naturally noisy molecular benchmarks and controlled label-flipping benchmarks show that MOLAR consistently outperforms representative baselines. Visualization analyses further show that MOLAR provides interpretable reliability and modality-evidence diagnostics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MOLAR, a noise-aware multimodal molecular representation learning framework designed to address the issue of noisy labels in molecular data.
AI.motivation: Noisy labels in molecular annotations can mislead models by propagating errors. This problem is further compounded in multimodal setups due to interactions between different modalities, such as graph-text fusion.
AI.method: MOLAR introduces a clean-property inference mechanism separating clean latent properties from noisy recorded labels using modality-specific evidence and a categorical label-observation channel.
AI.result: The model outperforms existing baselines on noisy-label molecular benchmarks and also provides interpretable diagnostics for label reliability and evidence contributions.
AI.conclusion: MOLAR effectively addresses the challenge of noisy labels in molecular representation learning, enhancing both performance and interpretability.

title: User as Engram: Internalizing Per-User Memory as Local Parametric Edits
summary:
Personal memory in a language model is two problems: content and reasoning skill. The brain keeps the two apart (a sparse, local engram in the hippocampus for each episode, a slow neocortex for the shared skills that interpret it), so a new fact need not overwrite everything else. Most personalization today keeps a user's facts outside the weights, in a natural-language memory file or a retrieval index. When facts are written into the model instead, the standard recipe is the per-user LoRA adapter, which does the opposite of the brain, folding content and skill into one global weight delta. Writing a user's facts as a LoRA contaminates text unrelated to them; writing the same facts as local Engram rows leaves it mathematically untouched, resulting in a roughly 33,000x smaller memory footprint.
  We therefore propose User as Engram: store a user's content as surgical edits to the hash-keyed memory table of an Engram model, and carry the reasoning skill in one shared adapter. This layered design matches per-user LoRA's direct recall while delivering 5.6x higher indirect-reasoning accuracy on average, and never makes a single user worse at reasoning than the untouched base. The edit is a glass box: writing a fact switches on its lookup at exactly the trigger, adds the value the answer needs, leaves every other position unchanged to the last bit, and fails if written into the wrong layer. Because different users' facts land in disjoint hash slots, their edits compose: many users live in one shared table at once, stacking additively and losslessly, where a per-user LoRA, a single global weight delta, admits only one. Upon retrieval, a per-user Engram table does not grow with the population the retriever must search, so past ~100 facts it overtakes a retrieval pipeline on a 2.5x larger model.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a novel approach for integrating personal memory into language models, separating user-specific content and reasoning skills using a shared adapter and local memory edits.
AI.motivation: To address the inefficiency and contamination issues in current methods like per-user LoRA adapters, and to create a system inspired by the brain’s structure for better memory management and reasoning.
AI.method: Using a user-specific hash-keyed memory table called User as Engram for storing content, coupled with a shared adapter for reasoning, with surgical edits ensuring memory integrity and compositionality.
AI.result: The method achieves direct recall, improves indirect reasoning accuracy by 5.6x compared to per-user LoRA, ensures no degradation in reasoning for any user, and scales efficiently with population and memory size.
AI.conclusion: The User as Engram approach separates content and reasoning, offering a scalable memory system that is additive, lossless, computationally efficient, and superior to existing personalization methods.

title: LLMs Struggle to Measure What Distinguishes Students of Different Proficiency Levels: A Study of Item Discrimination in Reading Comprehension Assessment
summary: Item discrimination is a fundamental psychometric property of educational assessment, which measures whether an item meaningfully distinguishes students with higher proficiency from students with lower proficiency. While various existing works have explored whether large language models (LLMs) can estimate item difficulty, it remains unclear whether they can capture item discrimination. In this work, we evaluate 42 proprietary and open-weight LLMs in zero-shot settings using two complementary approaches: direct discrimination prediction, where models explicitly estimate an item's discrimination value from its content, and response-based Classical Test Theory (CTT) calibration, where LLM answers are treated as synthetic student responses to compute discrimination scores. Our results show that direct prediction yields weak alignment with human-calibrated discrimination: the best-performing model reaches only a Spearman correlation of 0.152. Response-based CTT calibration provides a stronger but still limited signal, with the all-persona synthetic respondent pool reaching a Spearman correlation of 0.241. These findings highlight item discrimination as an open challenge for LLM-based psychometric evaluation: current LLMs contain non-random discrimination-relevant signal, but they do not yet reliably capture how assessment items distinguish human students.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) can effectively measure item discrimination in educational assessments, revealing weak alignment with human-calibrated metrics.
AI.motivation: The research aims to address whether large language models (LLMs) can capture a key psychometric property of educational assessments—item discrimination, which is critical for distinguishing between students with different proficiency levels.
AI.method: The study evaluates 42 proprietary and open-weight LLMs in zero-shot settings using two methods: (1) direct discrimination prediction, where models estimate discrimination values from item content, and (2) response-based Classical Test Theory (CTT) calibration, where simulated LLM responses are used to compute discrimination scores.
AI.result: The best-performing LLM in direct prediction shows weak alignment with human-calibrated discrimination (Spearman correlation: 0.152). Response-based CTT calibration yields slightly better results (Spearman correlation: 0.241).
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit some ability to capture discrimination-relevant signals, but they remain unreliable in effectively measuring how assessment items distinguish student proficiency. This represents an open challenge in LLM-based psychometric evaluation.

title: ZiMPedance: Impedance-Aware ZMP Modeling and Control for Payload Carrying with Quadruped Robots
summary: Load transportation with quadruped robots is strongly affected by the dynamics of the physical interface between the robot and the load. Passive spring-based arms reduce weight and complexity compared to active manipulators, but their spring-damper dynamics can introduce oscillatory forces that degrade locomotion stability. This paper derives an extended Zero Moment Point (ZMP) formulation that includes passive payload-interface dynamics, relating stiffness, damping, and payload mass to the stability margin. The analysis shows that underdamped configurations can resonate with locomotion harmonics. Based on this insight, we augment a Single Rigid Body Dynamics model with passive subsystem dynamics and integrate it into a Model Predictive Control framework. In simulation, the proposed controller reduces stability violations by up to $10\times$, from $7.0\%$ to $0.7\%$, and increase locomotion efficiency by lowering horizontal ground reaction force effort by up to $15\%$ compared to a nominal baseline. Hardware experiments with a $2\,\mathrm{kg}$ payload show stable locomotion under pull-release disturbances where the nominal controller fails. The same model also enables end-effector tracking through passive arm dynamics without direct arm actuation.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenges of load transportation in quadruped robots using passive spring-based arms, proposing a novel control model to improve stability and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the oscillatory forces and stability issues caused by the spring-damper dynamics of passive payload arms in quadruped robots.
AI.method: The authors develop an extended Zero Moment Point (ZMP) formulation and integrate the passive subsystem dynamics of a robot into a Model Predictive Control framework.
AI.result: In simulations, the controller reduces stability violations by up to $10\times$ and increases efficiency by reducing ground reaction force effort by up to $15\%$. Hardware experiments confirm stability under disturbances when a standard controller fails.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method improves both stability and efficiency in quadruped robots using passive arms and enables additional functionalities like end-effector tracking without direct actuation.

title: LandslideAgent with Multimodal LandslideBench: A Domain-Rule-Augmented Agent for Autonomous Landslide Identification and Analysis
summary: Intelligent landslide hazard interpretation is critical for disaster prevention, yet current paradigms struggle to simultaneously extract visual features and high-level geoscientific semantics, while general-purpose vision-language models (VLMs) suffer from perceptual limitations and domain hallucinations in complex geological scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose an instruction-driven agentic framework comprising three components. First, LandslideBench, a multimodal fine-grained dataset with seven subtype labels, high-resolution imagery, pixel-level masks, and high-quality textual descriptions, is constructed via multi-VLM cross-validation and interactive annotation. Then, LandslideVLM, a landslide-oriented VLM, is fine-tuned via LoRA on LandslideBench to enhance geological semantic understanding. Finally, LandslideAgent, a domain rule-enhanced agent taking LandslideVLM as its cognitive backbone, employs a dual-rule controller incorporating structured report metadata constraints and cross-validation identification constraints to regulate automated tool invocation. Experiments demonstrate that LandslideBench provides effective baselines across five mainstream models on fine-grained classification and semantic segmentation. LandslideVLM achieves accuracy improvements of 10.96%, 32.87%, and 15.91% on landslide discrimination, fine-grained classification, and semantic description quality, respectively. LandslideAgent further enables autonomous multi-source spatial data inference, realizing full-process intelligence for landslide identification and analysis.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a framework to improve geological landslide analysis by building a fine-grained dataset, fine-tuning a vision-language model (VLM), and creating an intelligent landslide identification agent.
AI.motivation: Current methods lack the ability to fully integrate visual features with geoscientific semantics, while general-purpose VLMs face perceptual and domain-specific challenges in analyzing complex geological hazards.
AI.method: The paper introduces three key components: LandslideBench, a fine-grained dataset created through multi-model validation and interactive annotation; LandslideVLM, a fine-tuned vision-language model optimized for landslide understanding; and LandslideAgent, an autonomous analysis tool using domain rules and dual-rule controllers.
AI.result: The framework achieves significant accuracy improvements: 10.96% in landslide discrimination, 32.87% in fine-grained classification, and 15.91% in semantic description quality. Additionally, it enables autonomous inferences across spatial data sources.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework advances landslide hazard analysis by integrating multimodal datasets, enhancing VLMs for geological understanding, and enabling autonomous, intelligent identification and analysis.

title: QC-GAN: A Parameter-Efficient Quaternion Conformer GAN for High-Fidelity Speech Enhancement
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures and 5 tables. Accepted at Interspeech2026
summary: We propose a parameter-efficient speech enhancement framework, Quaternion Conformer GAN (QC-GAN), which combines a Quaternion Conformer generator with MetricGAN-based training. The Hamilton product encodes the magnitude and phase via structured weight sharing, reducing the number of layer parameters while preserving their interdependencies. A metric-learning discriminator was employed to maximize perceptual quality by optimizing the approximate perceptual evaluation scores. On the VoiceBank+DEMAND dataset, QC-GAN achieved a Perceptual Evaluation of Speech Quality (PESQ) score of 3.48 with only 0.89M parameters, delivering a performance comparable to state-of-the-art models at less than half their size. A 35K-parameter variant achieved a PESQ score of 3.23, surpassing conventional methods with significantly fewer parameters. Evaluation on the DNS-Challenge 3 dataset further confirmed generalization to real-world conditions.

AI.tldr: QC-GAN is a parameter-efficient speech enhancement model with competitive PESQ scores and remarkable parameter reduction.
AI.motivation: There is a need for parameter-efficient speech enhancement models with high perceptual quality for practical use in real-world conditions.
AI.method: QC-GAN employs a Quaternion Conformer generator with Hamilton product and a metric-learning discriminator for MetricGAN-based training to improve perceptual evaluation scores.
AI.result: QC-GAN achieved a PESQ score of 3.48 using only 0.89M parameters, comparable to state-of-the-art models, while a 35K-parameter variant scored 3.23, surpassing traditional models.
AI.conclusion: QC-GAN effectively balances parameter efficiency and high perceptual quality, proving its suitability for real-world speech enhancement tasks.

title: P$^2$CE: Model-Agnostic Plausible Pareto-Optimal Counterfactual Explanations
comment: Under review in the Machine Learning journal
summary: The increasing use of machine learning algorithms in social applications has raised concerns about fairness and transparency, leading to the development of counterfactual explanations. These explanations supports individuals to understand and potentially alter unfavorable decisions in areas such as loan applications, job selections, and more, by providing actionable changes to input features that would lead to a desired outcome. Existing methods often struggle to balance feasibility, plausibility, and computational efficiency. To address this, we introduce P$^2$CE, an algorithm for generating plausible Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations, offering users a diverse set of optimal trade-offs between different notions of feasibility. P$^2$CE employs an auxiliary isolation forest outlier detector to ensure that explanations are in accordance with the data distribution and leverages SHAP values to obtain optimal results with short computing times, regardless of the underlying model. Our algorithm was empirically evaluated on three datasets, demonstrating superior performance in terms of both solution quality and computational efficiency compared to related techniques.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces P$^2$CE, a counterfactual explanation algorithm that balances feasibility, plausibility, and efficiency using an isolation forest and SHAP values.
AI.motivation: To tackle concerns of fairness and transparency in machine learning applications by improving existing counterfactual explanation methods that struggle with feasibility, plausibility, and efficiency.
AI.method: P$^2$CE algorithm uses an auxiliary isolation forest for plausible results and SHAP values for efficiency, providing users with Pareto-optimal counterfactual explanations.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three datasets shows P$^2$CE delivers better solution quality and computational efficiency compared to related methods.
AI.conclusion: P$^2$CE effectively supports fairness in machine learning decisions by generating efficient, plausible, and feasible counterfactual explanations.

title: TxBench-PP: Analyzing AI Agent Performance on Small-Molecule Preclinical Pharmacology
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) agents promise to accelerate drug discovery by compressing interpretation and decision-making loops, but practical deployment requires trusted evaluation on realistic program decisions. We introduce TherapeuticsBench Preclinical Pharmacology (TxBench-PP), a verifiable benchmark for small-molecule preclinical pharmacology and the first focused slice of a broader TherapeuticsBench effort across drug-discovery stages and therapeutic modalities. TxBench-PP tests whether agents can recover accurate conclusions from real-world assay data rather than memorized facts from literature. The benchmark contains 100 evaluations indexed by program stage, assay type, and task structure, spanning mechanism-of-action (MoA) and pharmacodynamic (PD) reasoning, compound-target engagement, causal target validation, developability and safety, and translational efficacy. Agents receive realistic workflow snapshots, inspect files in a coding environment, and return structured answers graded deterministically. Across 16 model-harness configurations, comprising 11 models and 4,800 trajectories, no system reliably recovered preclinical pharmacology decisions. The strongest configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 / Pi, passed 59.3\% of endpoint attempts (178/300; 95\% CI, 51.1-67.6), followed by GPT-5.5 / Pi at 55.3\% (166/300; 47.0-63.6).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces TxBench-PP, a benchmarking tool to evaluate AI in preclinical pharmacology tasks using real-world assay data, finding current AI systems insufficiently reliable.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to create a reliable benchmark to evaluate the ability of AI systems to handle real-world decision-making in preclinical pharmacology, beyond just recalling knowledge from literature.
AI.method: They developed TxBench-PP, a benchmark containing 100 evaluations, where AI agents must analyze realistic workflows and structured files to provide answers graded deterministically.
AI.result: Across 16 model configurations tested, no AI system reliably performed in preclinical pharmacology. The best model achieved 59.3% success in endpoint attempts, showing limitations of current systems.
AI.conclusion: Current AI systems are not yet dependable for making accurate decisions in preclinical pharmacology tasks, highlighting a gap for further development and reliability improvements.

title: Morpheus: A Morphology-Aware Neural Tokenizer and Word Embedder for Turkish
summary: Turkish is agglutinative: meaning is carried by morphemes, yet the subword tokenizers that drive modern language models split words by corpus statistics, fragmenting semantically loaded suffixes and -- in the case of WordPiece and rule-based analyzers -- failing to decode their output back to the original text. This paper presents \textbf{Morpheus}, a neural morpheme-boundary model for Turkish that is at once a lossless, morphology-aware tokenizer and a word-embedding producer. A differentiable Poisson-binomial dynamic program turns per-character boundary probabilities into soft morpheme memberships during training and exact segments at inference, with no string normalization, so $\mathrm{decode}(\mathrm{encode}(w)) = w$ holds by construction. Because the model is neural, the same forward pass that tokenizes also emits a structured word embedding. Among reversible tokenizers -- the only ones valid for generation -- Morpheus attains the lowest bits-per-character ($1.425$), roughly doubles the gold morphological alignment of the subword family (MorphScore macro-F1 $0.61$ vs.\ ${\sim}0.32$), and uses ${\sim}19\%$ less GPU memory than 64K-vocabulary subword tokenizers. As an embedder, frozen Morpheus vectors lead on lexical retrieval (root-family MAP $0.85$) and same-root verification (ROC-AUC $1.00$), surpassing the multilingual retriever BGE-M3 and BERTurk; on context- and inflection-dependent tasks (NER, case/number probing) the heavier contextual encoders remain ahead -- a trade-off we attribute to Morpheus's root-centric geometry. Code: https://github.com/lonewolf-rd/TurkishMorpheus; model: https://huggingface.co/lonewolflab/Morpheus-TR-50K; interactive demo: https://huggingface.co/spaces/lonewolflab/morpheus-tr-demo.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Morpheus, a neural morphology-aware tokenizer and embedder for Turkish, achieving lossless encoding-decoding, efficient GPU usage, and strong performance on morphological tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address limitations in existing subword tokenizers, especially for Turkish, an agglutinative language where semantically meaningful morphemes are fragmented, and decoding often fails to reconstruct original text.
AI.method: Morpheus employs a neural network with a differentiable Poisson-binomial dynamic program to compute soft morpheme memberships during training and exact segments during inference. It maintains lossless encoding-decoding and produces structured word embeddings alongside tokenization.
AI.result: Morpheus achieves the lowest bits-per-character (1.425) among reversible tokenizers, improves morphological alignment (Macro-F1 MorphScore 0.61), and reduces GPU memory usage by ~19% compared to 64K-vocabulary subword tokenizers. It also excels in lexical retrieval and same-root verification tasks.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates Morpheus's effectiveness as a morphology-aware tokenizer and embedder for Turkish, bridging the gap between lossless tokenization, efficient resource usage, and strong performance on morphology-driven tasks, though with limitations on context-dependent tasks.

title: C-ARC: Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering for Non-Repetitive LiDAR Sensors
comment: Submitted to IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters. This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication. 8 pages, 7 figures
summary: Real-time LiDAR clustering identifies structures in point clouds, which is an essential prerequisite for many mobile robotics algorithms. Current methods are mostly developed for repetitive mechanical LiDAR sensors. Recently, the use of non-repetitive LiDAR sensors is strongly increasing due to their small cost and form factor. Such non-repetitive Risley prism-based sensors violate two key assumptions of repetitive mechanical sensors: structured scan lines and well-defined frame boundaries. Their Rhodonea-curve trajectories produce non-uniform point distributions, and the absence of a rotation cycle renders conventional scan line indexing inapplicable. To meet such new requirements, we developed C-ARC, a Continuous-Adaptive Range Clustering framework that maintains a persistent dual-graph over a sliding window, decoupling high-frequency point insertion from on-demand cluster retrieval. This is crucial for key functionalities like SLAM or tracking. An adaptive range grid resolution mechanism calibrates grid dimensions at initialization using an exponential control loop, balancing the sparsity-collision trade-off without prior knowledge of the scanning pattern. Implemented as an open-sourced single-threaded C++17 library, C-ARC produces real-time cluster output at 20 Hz on commodity hardware for the Livox Mid-360. Evaluation on the Livox Avia identifies unbounded cell occupancy as the primary limitation for sensors with strongly concentrated scan patterns. The adaptive resolution mechanism additionally improves clustering quality for existing grid-based methods on non-repetitive data.

AI.tldr: This paper presents C-ARC, a clustering framework tailored for non-repetitive LiDAR sensors, enabling real-time performance and adaptability to these sensors' unique characteristics.
AI.motivation: The increasing use of non-repetitive LiDAR sensors has introduced challenges due to their non-uniform point distributions and lack of structured scan lines and frame boundaries, which conventional methods cannot handle effectively.
AI.method: C-ARC deploys a dual-graph persistent representation over a sliding window, coupled with an adaptive range grid resolution mechanism calibrated through an exponential control loop, enabling dynamic adjustment to scanning patterns.
AI.result: C-ARC achieves real-time clustering at 20 Hz on standard hardware for Livox Mid-360 and improves the clustering quality of existing methods on non-repetitive data. However, sensors with highly concentrated scan patterns face limitations in cell occupancy.
AI.conclusion: C-ARC is a robust, open-source, single-threaded solution that addresses the challenges of non-repetitive LiDAR clustering, highlighting adaptability and practical performance, though with limitations under specific sensor patterns.

title: BrainFusionNet: a deep learning and XAI model to understand local, global, and sequential features of MRI images for improved brain tumour detection
summary: The noise of Magnetic Resonance Imaging MRI poses challenges for Deep Learning DL when tumor boundaries are obscured tumor location and appearance are complex Therefore we develop BrainFusionNet that combines Convolutional Neural Networks CNNs Vision Transformers ViT and Gated Recurrent Units GRUs to extract spatial contextual and sequential features from MRI images for improved brain tumor classification Furthermore explainable AI such as SHAP LIME and GradCAM are integrated to visualise and highlight image regions that contribute to BrainFusionNets decisionmaking process The proposed BrainFusionNet model is evaluated on two publicly available MRI datasets Kfold validation suggests 98 accuracy on both datasets The model was compared with the six stateoftheart SOTA CNNs and transfer learning Among the SOTA CNNs DenseNet121 and VGG16 achieved the highest accuracy of 96 The novelty of BrainFusionNet is that the hybrid model effectively extracts local and global features from MRI images even in smallscale tumor regions and small tumor sizes The model has a balanced sequential CNN architecture to capture lowlevel and deeperlayer features a customized ViT that captures local features stabilizes gradient flow and reduces the risk of vanishing gradients during MRI image training The CNN and ViT outputs are fed into a GRU for final classification Furthermore we analyze pixel intensities to determine whether MRI image quality affects image classification Our findings are very novel in image interpretation as we found that the distribution of pixel intensities in MRI images affects DL performance

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BrainFusionNet, a hybrid model combining CNNs, Vision Transformers, and GRUs, achieving 98% accuracy in brain tumor classification using MRI data.
AI.motivation: The challenge addressed is the noise in MRI data that complicates tumor boundary recognition and classification due to complex tumor appearance and location.
AI.method: BrainFusionNet integrates CNNs, Vision Transformers, and GRUs for feature extraction, and employs explainable AI tools to highlight key image regions influencing decisions. Sequential features are used alongside spatial and contextual data.
AI.result: The model achieves 98% accuracy using K-fold validation on two public MRI datasets. It surpasses existing CNN architectures like DenseNet121 and VGG16, which achieved 96% accuracy.
AI.conclusion: BrainFusionNet's combination of sequential, local, and global feature extraction enhances performance, even for small tumor sizes. It introduces stability in gradient flow and reduces vanishing gradients. Additionally, the study found that MRI pixel intensity distribution impacts classification accuracy.

title: Online Distributional Prediction via Latent Cluster Geometry Under Drift and Corruption
summary:
Online learning in non-stationary streams is often formulated as tracking a point estimate, but many applications require predicting the full data-generating distribution. We study online distributional prediction under drift and adversarial corruption. Our approach represents each candidate law through a latent cluster geometry: a variable-size configuration of centers that organizes probability mass and induces a predictive distribution. A Gibbs quasi-posterior over these configurations yields an online predictor by posterior averaging, and the resulting variable-dimensional posterior can be sampled with reversible-jump MCMC. The method therefore avoids specifying a parametric streaming law while retaining a structured latent space for uncertainty, regularization, and comparison.
  We evaluate performance by cumulative Wasserstein-1 regret against the time-varying true law. The analysis separates two effects: corruption perturbs the loss-based posterior update, whereas drift makes long-horizon posterior memory stale. We address the latter with a restarted variant that temporally localizes the same quasi-Bayesian update. The resulting high-probability bounds decompose into a PAC-Bayesian complexity term, a corruption-sensitive posterior perturbation term, and a dynamic optimal-transport term driven by \(A_T^{\mathrm{OT}}=\sum_{t=2}^T W_2^2(p_{t-1}^*,p_t^*)\). Under bounded support, stable latent geometry, predictive-map regularity, oracle realizability, localized restart windows, sublinear transport action, and sublinear corruption budget, the restarted predictor achieves sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret. These guarantees require no parametric model for the stream, drift mechanism, or corruption process.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a method for online distributional prediction in non-stationary data streams, focusing on cumulative Wasserstein regret under drift and corruption.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the challenges of predicting the full data-generating distribution in non-stationary streams, which goes beyond tracking point estimates, particularly under drift and adversarial corruption.
AI.method: The method represents candidate laws with a latent cluster geometry organized as variable-size configurations of centers, which induce predictive distributions. It uses a Gibbs quasi-posterior and reversible-jump MCMC for nonparametric online prediction and introduces a restarted variant to address memory staleness caused by drift.
AI.result: The approach yields high-probability bounds for performance evaluation, including sublinear cumulative Wasserstein regret, under assumptions of stable latent geometry, regularized predictive mapping, realizability, and bounded corruption and transport action.
AI.conclusion: The proposed nonparametric method successfully tackles distributional prediction in dynamic and corrupted streams without requiring parametric models, offering provable theoretical guarantees.

title: X+Slides: Benchmarking Audience-Conditioned Slide Generation
summary: Automatically generating slide decks from source documents is an important application of large language models (LLMs). Existing benchmarks primarily assess slide completeness and technical depth, while overlooking the target audience as a critical real-world factor. For instance, specialists demand rigorous proofs, whereas decision-makers prioritize actionable conclusions. To bridge this gap, we introduce X+Slides, a benchmark specifically designed for audience-conditioned slide generation. Built on a diverse corpus spanning 113 topics and seven presentation scenes, X+Slides employs a dynamic evaluation framework constructed from 8,133 deduplicated, source-grounded probes. By assigning audience-specific utility weights to the same source-grounded probes, X+Slides reports four complementary metrics: Audience Coverage measures how much audience-essential information is conveyed, Domain-wise Coverage shows which information types are covered, Efficiency measures delivered utility per unit of attention cost, and Correctness verifies whether slide claims are supported by the source. Experiments on DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, and NotebookLM show that current systems can recover a substantial but still incomplete part of audience-essential information: at $τ_A=0.7$, DeepPresenter reaches a best Audience Coverage of 0.714, SlideTailor reaches 0.594, and the NotebookLM ablation reaches 0.853 while showing clear grounding differences. These results indicate that visual quality and broad topic coverage should not be treated as evidence support without source-grounded evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces X+Slides, a benchmark for audience-conditioned slide generation, emphasizing target audience needs in automated slide construction and presenting metrics including Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, and Correctness.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks in slide generation overlook the importance of tailoring content for specific audiences. The paper aims to address this gap by proposing a benchmark that evaluates the alignment of slides to audience expectations.
AI.method: The authors developed X+Slides, a benchmark drawn from a diverse corpus of 113 topics and seven presentation scenarios. It uses 8,133 source-grounded probes with audience-specific utility weights, enabling the measurement of metrics like Audience Coverage, Domain-wise Coverage, Efficiency, and Correctness.
AI.result: Evaluations on three systems (DeepPresenter, SlideTailor, NotebookLM) show varying levels of success in audience-specific slide generation, with Audience Coverage scores reaching up to 0.853. Differences in grounding quality among systems were observed.
AI.conclusion: Current systems only partially fulfill the goal of audience-essential information recovery, highlighting the need for audience-specific and source-grounded evaluations in automated slide generation systems.

title: LegalWorld: A Life-Cycle Interactive Environment for Legal Agents
summary: Civil litigation is inherently a life-cycle process: what a lawyer drafts on day one constrains what unfolds at trial months later. Yet existing legal benchmarks evaluate isolated subtasks, and prior legal-agent simulators reinitialize each scenario from shared ground truth, leaving cross-stage causal dependencies unmodeled. We present LegalWorld, a life-cycle interactive environment that models Chinese civil litigation as a causally connected state chain of five stages (seven sub-scenarios), grounded in 75,309 paired Chinese civil judgments. We pair it with reusable infrastructure (local memory, global case memory, a Skill/Tool library) that keeps each dispute consistent across its full life cycle. Building on this environment, we construct LongJud-Bench to evaluate agent capability across all five connected stages. 18,992 ratings from 217 legal-background evaluators confirm that LegalWorld trajectories are procedurally faithful and role-consistent; and a capability-level cross-model evaluation reveals sharp divergences that aggregate scores cannot expose, with no single backbone leading across consultation, drafting, and courtroom advocacy. Detailed resources will be released publicly.

AI.tldr: LegalWorld models Chinese civil litigation as a causally connected state chain, providing a comprehensive framework for evaluating legal agents' capabilities across full litigation life cycles.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of prior legal-agent simulators and benchmarks that fail to model cross-stage causal dependencies in civil litigation.
AI.method: Developed a life-cycle interactive simulation, LegalWorld, based on 75,309 Chinese civil judgments, paired with infrastructure ensuring dispute consistency throughout its stages.
AI.result: 18,992 ratings from legal experts validated LegalWorld's procedural and role-faithfulness. It exposed capability divergences among agents across different litigation stages.
AI.conclusion: LegalWorld and its LongJud-Bench enable a deeper evaluation of legal agents, demonstrating the necessity of life-cycle consistency and revealing performance gaps among models.

title: A High-accuracy Event-based Underwater SLAM System
summary: While event cameras offer immense potential for underwater SLAM, existing Time Surface (TS)-based methods prove highly unreliable when deployed underwater. Fluctuating camera velocities severely degrade TS imaging quality, while wide stereo baselines and repetitive underwater textures induce critical matching failures, frequently triggering system failure. To overcome these challenges, we develop the first high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system. A structure-aware metric for TS is designed based on structure tensor coherence and gradients to quantitatively evaluate TS structural information density. By decoupling the optimal TS generation into two distinct stages based on system initialization, Bayesian Optimization(BO) first predicts an optimal prior TS sequentially before initialization while we set an asynchronous online local searching method periodically to obtain appropriate TS in real-time during the tracking stage. We use the prior disparity to guarantee precise data association and "latest-observation-first'' triangulation mechanism to realize stable triangulation. As a benchmark for these solutions and a resource for the community, we also contribute UWE, the first high-quality real-world underwater event dataset containing variable camera motions, complex textures and different trajectory features. Extensive evaluations on public datasets and UWE show the competitive accuracy performance of the proposed SLAM system compared to the state-of-the-art event-based method. The code and data will be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a novel high-accuracy event-based underwater stereo SLAM system addressing the limitations of Time Surface methods caused by underwater conditions like fluctuating velocities and repetitive textures. It introduces UWE, a dataset and metric-based advancements to improve accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing Time Surface methods struggle underwater due to fluctuating camera velocities and repetitive textures, leading to unreliable SLAM performance. This paper seeks to address these challenges to unlock the potential of event cameras in underwater environments.
AI.method: The authors design a structure-aware TS metric using coherence and gradients to evaluate structural density. They decouple TS generation into two stages: Bayesian Optimization for prior TS prediction and asynchronous online local search for real-time TS refinement. Moreover, prior disparity and triangulation mechanisms ensure data association and system stability.
AI.result: The proposed SLAM system achieves competitive accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods, validated through evaluations on public datasets and UWE, the newly introduced underwater event dataset.
AI.conclusion: The paper contributes significant advancements in underwater SLAM by overcoming traditional TS limitations, providing a robust event-based solution alongside a high-quality dataset and open-source code for broader community use.

title: Moving Beyond Diversity: Visual Token Pruning as Subspace Reconstruction for Efficient VLMs
summary: Despite their remarkable performance, Vision Language Models (VLMs) incur substantial computational overhead due to the large number of visual tokens. While diversity maximization has become a dominant strategy for token reduction, existing methods rely on cosine-based normalized similarity that discards magnitude information, failing to faithfully approximate the original feature representation and leading to suboptimal performance, particularly on compositional multi-skill reasoning tasks. In this paper, we introduce SPARE, a subspace reconstruction method that reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem and explicitly minimizes reconstruction error. By iteratively selecting tokens with large projection residuals, SPARE performs reconstruction-driven pruning beyond angular diversity. Moreover, we reveal a counterintuitive anti-relevance phenomenon: tokens with lower image-text relevance score can better preserve contextual information. Based on this finding, we incorporate anti-relevance into SPARE as an additional selection criterion to promote context-aware token selection. Extensive experiments across multiple VLMs and benchmarks demonstrate that SPARE consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, with strong gains on compositional tasks. When applied to LLaVA, SPARE removes up to 94% of visual tokens while retaining 95% of the baseline performance, all in a fully training-free manner.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPARE, a method that substantially reduces the computational burden of Vision Language Models (VLMs) by efficiently pruning visual tokens through a reconstruction-based approach, achieving strong performance even on complex tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision Language Models are computation-heavy due to an excessive number of visual tokens. Current diversity-based token reduction methods rely on cosine similarity and discard magnitude information, leading to suboptimal performance, especially on compositional tasks.
AI.method: The method, SPARE, reformulates token pruning as a column subset selection problem with an explicit goal of minimizing reconstruction error. It iteratively selects tokens based on projection residuals and introduces an anti-relevance criterion to promote context preservation.
AI.result: SPARE achieves state-of-the-art performance across various VLMs and benchmarks, excelling in compositional reasoning tasks. It demonstrates the ability to prune up to 94% of visual tokens in LLaVA systems while maintaining 95% of baseline performance, entirely training-free.
AI.conclusion: SPARE showcases a novel solution to token pruning for VLMs by focusing on reconstruction-driven selection and highlighting the role of anti-relevance. This leads to significant computational efficiency gains without sacrificing performance.

title: Strategic Feature Selection
summary: When algorithmic predictors inform resource allocation in high-stakes domains such as healthcare, these predictors must account for strategic manipulation of input features. The typical solution is to redesign the predictor itself to explicitly account for strategic interactions. In practice, however, decision makers are often constrained to adjusting coarser levers within existing prediction pipelines. For example, healthcare organizations often select which features to exclude based on perceived manipulability, while using standard regularization procedures to shrink the coefficients of retained features. In this work, we initiate a formal study of strategic classification through feature selection and its interaction with ridge regularization. Our main finding is that excluding individual features based on their manipulability alone is generally suboptimal. We provide a fine-grained characterization of the performance of a feature subset under optimal regularization, yielding new insights for policy design. Motivated by this characterization, we develop a practical algorithm for jointly choosing the feature set and the level of ridge regularization. Through a real-world case study on a healthcare payments benchmark, we illustrate how our algorithm can guide the design of coarse policy levers in practice. Our results provide a principled, practical framework for mitigating the effects of strategic behavior in algorithmic decision-making systems.

AI.tldr: Strategic feature exclusion based solely on manipulability is suboptimal in algorithmic predictions. A joint consideration of feature selection and ridge regularization offers better outcomes.
AI.motivation: High-stakes decision-making domains like healthcare require robust predictors that can handle strategic input manipulations without compromising resource allocation fairness or efficiency.
AI.method: The study proposes a formal analysis of feature selection in the presence of strategic behavior, assesses performance under optimal ridge regularization, and introduces a novel algorithm for jointly optimizing feature selection and regularization intensity.
AI.result: The work demonstrates the suboptimality of excluding features purely on manipulability. It establishes principles and designs an algorithm that balances feature set choice and regularization for better policy outcomes.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide a coherent framework for designing decision systems that effectively mitigate strategic manipulation while ensuring practical applicability, demonstrated via a healthcare case study.

title: Signature filtering: a lightweight enhancement for statistical watermark detection in large language models
summary: Statistical watermarks help organizations attribute large language model (LLM) outputs, yet existing detectors often struggle when watermark signals are weak, texts are repetitive, or watermarks are edited. We propose signature filtering, a detection-time module that enhances watermark detection without modifying watermark embedding and text generation. It learns a small set of ``signature'' tokens whose presence makes watermark tests unreliable, and removes these tokens before detection. The signatures are obtained by solving a mixed-integer linear program on a small training set, with constraints that maximize the true positive rate. We additionally derive finite-sample and asymptotic bounds under several attacker models (color-blind, color-adaptive, and distributionally correlated). On four well-known watermark families (Kgw, Sweet, Unigram, Exp), four benchmark corpora (C4, MBPP, HumanEval, Code-Search-Net), and six LLMs (Opt-1.3b, Opt-6.7b, Llama2-13b, Llama3.1-8b, Qwen2.5-14b, Phi-3-medium-14b), 2- and 3-gram signatures raise detection rates in weak-signal and low-entropy settings from 8~31% without filtering to 78~99% with filtering, while keeping false positives controllable and often negligible. In stress tests where we scramble sentences and perturb 25~50% of tokens by dilution, deletions, and substitutions, 2-gram filters for Kgw-style watermarks preserve most of the clean-text detection gains, often matching or outperforming the advanced WinMax watermark detector. Signature filtering thus provides a simple, scalable, and model-agnostic add-on to strengthen watermark-based provenance checks for LLM text in information processing workflows.

AI.tldr: Signature filtering improves statistical watermark detection by removing 'signature' tokens, increasing true positive rates significantly, even in weak-signal and manipulated settings.
AI.motivation: Existing watermark detectors for large language model (LLM) outputs often fail in cases of weak watermark signals, repetitive text, or deliberately edited watermarks. This paper aims to enhance detection robustness without altering watermark embedding or text generation.
AI.method: The authors propose 'signature filtering,' a detection module that identifies and removes 'signature' tokens, which degrade watermark tests' reliability. This is achieved via a mixed-integer linear program optimized to maximize true positive rates. Performance is also theoretically analyzed under various attacker models.
AI.result: The proposed approach significantly improves detection rates (from 8–31% to 78–99%) across different watermarks, datasets, and LLMs, while maintaining low false positive rates. It also retains robustness under stress tests involving severe text modifications.
AI.conclusion: Signature filtering offers a practical, scalable, and model-agnostic method to boost the reliability of watermark-based attribution systems, making it a valuable tool for LLM text provenance in various contexts.

title: NeSyCat Torch: A Differentiable Tensor Implementation of Categorical Semantics for Neurosymbolic Learning
summary:
Neurosymbolic semantics is fragmented: classical, fuzzy, probabilistic and neural systems each define truth by their own inductive rules. NeSyCat, extending ULLER, subsumes them under a single inductive definition of truth, parametric in a strong monad and an aggregation structure on truth-values. NeSyCat has so far lacked an account of predicates and functions learned by neural networks.
  We provide NeSyCat Torch as the missing link and interpret computational symbols via neural networks, implementing the framework in probabilistic programming and tensor-based backends. We use the distribution monad for reference semantics and metric evaluation, and complement it by a monad for numerically stable, differentiable training: the lazy log-tensor monad over the log-semiring. For efficient training in batches, we furthermore employ a batch monad. The axioms are the source code: written once in monad-based do-notation, monadic bind performs marginalisation, lazily pruning unneeded branches.
  On MNIST addition, our HaskTorch, JAX, and PyTorch implementations outperform LTN and DeepProbLog in speed and accuracy, while achieving nearly the accuracy of DeepStochLog. However, unlike DeepStochLog, we stay in a uniform framework that applies to many first-order NeSy approaches. Namely, the construction is parametric in the monad; instantiating it with, e.g., the Giry monad extends the approach to continuous probability (working out a neural representation here is left for future work).

AI.tldr: NeSyCat Torch extends NeSyCat by integrating predicates and functions learned via neural networks, providing implementations that outperform existing systems in speed and accuracy for probabilistic and tensor-based backends.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in neurosymbolic semantics by unifying truth definitions using NeSyCat.
AI.method: Introduced NeSyCat Torch with computational symbols interpreted via neural networks, leveraging monads for training, evaluation, and efficient batching.
AI.result: Achieved superior speed and accuracy compared to LTN and DeepProbLog, and near-parity with DeepStochLog on MNIST addition task.
AI.conclusion: NeSyCat Torch provides a generalized, efficient framework for neurosymbolic systems, encouraging broader applicability and further extensions.

title: Output Vector Editing for Memorization Mitigation in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models memorize and reproduce sequences from their training data, creating privacy, copyright, and security risks. Existing neuron-level mitigation methods equate editing with zeroing out neuron activations, but the activation only controls whether a neuron engages; the output vector is what writes to the residual stream and, through superposition, encodes multiple features. We propose output vector editing, a constrained-optimization weight edit that locates a small set of MLP neurons responsible for a memorized continuation and minimally modifies their output vectors to introduce a distractor in vocabulary space, redirecting their residual-stream contributions while leaving activations unchanged. Evaluating on four models from 360M to 7B parameters (SmolLM-360M, OLMo-1B, OLMo-7B, Llama2-7B), we center on OLMo-7B (whose open weights and pretraining corpus enable systematic mining) and mine 6831 memorized sequences, achieving up to 87.9% suppression. The 2.7$\times$ gap over zero ablation on the same located neurons shows the suppression comes from the output-vector edit, not localization alone. Four edit modes span a spectrum from aggressive suppression to minimal redirection; in ensemble they cover 96.5% of memorized sequences, while our recommended single-mode configuration reaches 81.5% with no catastrophic locality failures. We further identify a mechanistic boundary at ${\sim}14%$ of sequences unreachable by MLP-only editing; while these failures are not attention-driven overall, ablating the top contributing attention heads recovers 60--64% of them, with stronger recovery on continuations that copy tokens from the prefix, positioning attention as a complementary fallback rather than a primary mechanism. Edit mode ordering and the success-locality trade-off transfer across all four models, with success rates scaling with model size rather than family.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the privacy risks of memorization in large language models by introducing a novel editing mechanism for suppressing memorized continuations, achieving high suppression rates without catastrophic failures.
AI.motivation: Large language models unintentionally memorize training data sequences, raising significant privacy, copyright, and security risks.
AI.method: The authors propose a constrained-optimization technique called output vector editing, which minimally modifies MLP neuron outputs responsible for memorization while introducing vocabulary distractors and maintaining neuron activations.
AI.result: The approach achieves up to 87.9% suppression of memorized sequences on OLMo-7B, outperforms zero ablation methods by a 2.7x margin, and shows scalability with model size. Mechanical boundaries and complementary attention-based fallback methods are also identified.
AI.conclusion: Output vector editing effectively suppresses memorized sequences in large language models, demonstrating scalability and success-locality trade-offs that extend across multiple models.

title: Object-Centric Residual RL for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real VLA Enhancement
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models can generalize across diverse manipulation tasks, but their imitation-learning-based policies remain brittle in precise physical interactions due to compounding execution errors; Can a reinforcement learning policy trained purely in simulation improve the robustness of real-world VLAs zero-shot? Residual RL, which learns a corrective policy on top of a frozen VLA, offers a natural framework, but existing approaches face a fundamental sim-to-real dilemma: privileged-state methods require lossy distillation for deployment; image-based methods suffer from the visual domain gap; and real-world RL is costly and unsafe. We propose an object-centric residual RL framework that refines VLA actions using object poses, enabling a compact observation space that transfers consistently between simulation and reality. To align the two domains, we additionally replay the same teleoperation demonstrations in simulation to train a sim counterpart of the real-world VLA. The residual RL policy is trained only in simulation with pose noise injection and dropout, and transfers zero-shot to the real robot. Across five manipulation tasks on a real Franka Research 3 (FR3) robot, our method improves the success rate from 42% to 76% zero-shot, and the improved rollouts can be further reused to retrain the base VLA for self-improvement without additional teleoperation. Project page: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/research/articles/object-centric-residual-rl/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an object-centric residual RL framework to improve Vision-Language-Action (VLA) model performance in physical interactions by leveraging simulation-trained policies that transfer to real-world robots zero-shot, effectively enhancing task success rates.
AI.motivation: To address the brittleness of VLA policies in precise physical interactions, and overcome issues like sim-to-real gaps, costly and unsafe real-world reinforcement learning (RL).
AI.method: The framework uses residual RL trained in simulation with object poses as observations, combined with methods like pose noise injection and dropout. It aligns simulation and real-world domains by replaying teleoperation demonstrations in simulation.
AI.result: The method improves task success rates of the VLA model on a real robotic setup (FR3 robot) from 42% to 76% zero-shot.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enhances the robustness of VLA models in real-world interactions and facilitates further self-improvement without additional costly teleoperation efforts.

title: Multi-Class Brain Tumor Classification Using Advanced Deep Learning Models: A Comparative Study
summary: Despite recent advancements in deep learning, accurately classifying brain tumors from MRI images continues to pose challenges. In this research, we present a comprehensive evaluation of five different convolutional neural networks (CNN) architectures, including a customized baseline model and four pre-trained models - for use in classifying multi-class brain tumors using a clinically-sourced dataset of approximately 10,000 MRI images. We have utilized five different architectures; VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, and EfficientNetB0, which were all tested and trained within an identical experimental framework. Performance was measured by both overall accuracy and tumor-wise recall as a means to measure the clinically-relevant performance of each architecture. We found that EfficientNetB0 had the best overall classification accuracy at 95%, when compared to the other architectures tested; specifically VGG16 (94.37%), VGG19 (92.29%), DenseNet121 (90.91%) and the customized CNN (78.00%). An especially important finding of our research was the considerable improvement in detecting meningiomas; specifically, while simple CNNs could detect meningiomas with a recall rate of approximately 20%, EfficientNetB0 was able to detect meningiomas with a recall rate of 89%. Meningiomas are often difficult to detect because they can appear very subtly on MRI images. Additionally, an interesting finding was that the deeper VGG19 performed worse than the shallower VGG16. This indicates that in many cases the architectural efficiency of a CNN model may be more important than its depth when working with medical images. Overall, EfficientNetB0 appears to provide the optimal trade-off between classification accuracy, number of parameters used in the model and clinically meaningful performance.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates five CNN architectures for classifying brain tumors from MRI images, finding EfficientNetB0 to perform the best with 95% accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of accurately classifying multi-class brain tumors from MRI images due to their clinical and diagnostic significance.
AI.method: Five CNN architectures (VGG16, VGG19, DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, and a customized CNN) were evaluated on a clinical dataset of ~10,000 MRI images using identical experimental settings, measuring their accuracy and tumor-wise recall.
AI.result: EfficientNetB0 achieved the highest classification accuracy (95%), with significant improvements in detecting meningiomas (recall rate: 89%) compared to other models.
AI.conclusion: EfficientNetB0 offers the optimal balance between classification accuracy, parameter efficiency, and clinical utility, outperforming deeper or less efficient architectures for brain tumor detection in MRI images.

title: Balanced Twins: Causal Inference on Time Series with Hidden Confounding
summary: Accurately estimating treatment effects in time series is essential for evaluating interventions in real-world applications, especially when treatment assignment is biased by unobserved factors. In many practical settings, interventions are adopted at different times across individuals, leading to staggered treatment exposure and heterogeneous pre-treatment histories. In such cases, aggregating outcome trajectories across treated units is ill-defined, making individual treatment effect (ITE) estimation a prerequisite for reliable causal inference. We therefore study the problem of estimating the average treatment effect for the treated (ATT) by first recovering individual-level counterfactuals. We introduce a neural framework that learns simultaneously low-dimensional latent representations of individual time series and propensity scores. These estimates are then used to approximate the individual treatment effects through a flexible matching procedure that avoids classical convexity constraints commonly used in synthetic control methods. By operating at the individual level, our approach naturally accommodates staggered interventions and improves counterfactual estimation under latent bias, without relying on explicit temporal modeling assumptions. We illustrate our approach on both real-world energy consumption data and clinical time series, including high-frequency electricity demand-response programs and semi-synthetic data for individuals in intensive care unit (ICU), where hidden confounding, staggered treatment adoption, and non-stationary dynamics are prevalent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a neural framework for estimating the average treatment effect in staggered and biased time series by recovering individual-level counterfactuals and employing a flexible matching approach.
AI.motivation: Staggered and biased treatment assignments in time series complicate causal inference, particularly when aggregating data with heterogeneous pre-treatment histories and unobserved confounders.
AI.method: A neural framework was developed that learns latent representations of time series and propensity scores simultaneously. It uses these representations to approximate treatment effects via a flexible matching procedure, avoiding traditional convexity assumptions.
AI.result: The approach effectively handles staggered interventions and latent biases, improving counterfactual estimations. Applications are demonstrated on energy consumption and ICU clinical data, confirming its efficacy.
AI.conclusion: By focusing on individual-level treatment effects, the proposed approach mitigates issues of bias and heterogeneous data, offering a robust solution for causal inference in complex real-world settings.

title: Rethinking Reward Supervision: Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation
summary: Post-training of reasoning language models is commonly driven by supervised distillation and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Distillation often relies on chain-of-thought annotations that are expensive to obtain and may themselves be noisy, incomplete, or partially incorrect; even when the final solution is correct, an imperfect rationale can interfere with learning. Reinforcement learning with verified rewards, on the other hand, typically compresses evaluative feedback into a scalar signal, obscuring which aspects of a response should be improved. We propose \textbf{Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation}, a framework that incorporates rubrics as structured, fine-grained feedback for on-policy self-distillation. Our method conditions the teacher model on criterion-level rubrics and uses it to provide token-level guidance on the student's own sampled trajectories. This design avoids treating a single reference rationale as the sole supervision target. Instead, rubrics specify what a strong response should satisfy, enabling more fine-grained credit assignment over the reasoning process than scalar reward optimization. We instantiate this framework with a two-stage pipeline that first learns to generate task-specific rubrics and then trains a rubric-guided reasoner. We evaluate on a diverse suite of science reasoning benchmarks and results show that rubric-conditioned self-distillation effectively converts rubric-level criteria into token-level guidance over the reasoning process, surpassing GRPO by 1.0 points and OPSD by 0.9 points on average.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Rubric-Conditioned Self-Distillation, a method to improve reasoning language model training by incorporating structured rubric-based feedback.
